entries.title: Blackhawks crumble late against Devils as defensive issues continue
entries.summary: <p>NEWARK, N.J. — As the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a> work through some serious defensive issues, they need stellar goaltending to have any chance to win.</p><p>And that’s just to have a chance, as evidenced by the fact Spencer Knight’s sharpness in net Sunday still wasn’t enough to prevent a 5-3 loss to the Devils.</p><p>Knight saved 35 of 39 shots in a bounce-back performance after a shakier night Thursday against the Flyers. But the Hawks weren’t able to hold 1-0, 2-1 or 3-2 leads before falling apart late.</p><p>Devils star Jack Hughes, who amassed a whopping 19 shot attempts, blew past Alex Vlasic for the game-winner with 5:09 left, just 19 seconds after Dougie Hamilton had tied the game.</p><p>"Our goalies have been amazing all season," Ryan Donato said. "It’s tough to see them put in those situations where we’re giving up that many chances."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Jack Hughes wheels in and gives the Devils the lead: <a href="https://t.co/YDw8hxHX8R">pic.twitter.com/YDw8hxHX8R</a></p>— BHF (@BlackhawksFocus) <a href="https://twitter.com/BlackhawksFocus/status/2038426967544885602?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 30, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>The Hawks have now conceded 49, 33, 47, 42, 39 and 40 shots on goal in their last six games, winning just one. </p><p>It's easy to focus exclusively on the defense as the culprit there — and the defense has indeed been poor — but a lack of possession time in all three zones is simply giving opponents too many opportunities to exploit those defensive cracks.</p><p>Coach Jeff Blashill believed the Hawks' approach, at least, was better Sunday.</p><p>"Our oldest center was 22, and our 'D' could be playing in the NCAA Tournament right now," Blashill said. "They're going to make some mistakes, and we're going to have to be patient.</p><p>"It's part of where we're at. We've got to keep grinding through and making sure, as we go through some of these frustrating moments where we gave up a lead late, that we're learning. There's nothing wrong with failure if you learn from it."</p><p>Frank Nazar, who recorded his first career two-goal game, expressed a similar sentiment at the end of a physically and emotionally tiring four-game road trip.</p><p>"There's no doubt in my mind that we're going to become a better hockey team," Nazar said.</p><p>"I have full belief in our team. I think our team can skate with any other team in the league. I love these guys, and I think everybody in here has the heart and skill to do it right. It's just, sometimes stuff like that happens and we've got to learn from it."</p><h3><b>New ideas</b></h3><p>Blashill experimented with a few interesting lineup changes. He put Anton Frondell at center in his fourth NHL game — even though he rarely played center in Sweden. He also deployed a five-forward top power-play unit quarterbacked by Connor Bedard.</p><p>Both yielded dividends. Frondell tallied two assists, and the power play converted for the first time in nine games during a five-on-three.</p>
entries.author: Ben Pope

entries.title: Shota Imanaga bitten by long ball in first start of season
entries.summary: <p>The Cubs and left-hander Shota Imanaga were on the brink of dissolution last fall before reconciling.</p><p>Both sides showed a willingness to try again.</p><p>But as anyone who has experienced a rough patch in a relationship can tell you, this might take a little time before trust is reestablished.</p><p>The last thing the Cubs or Imanaga wanted to see is what took place in the first inning Sunday, when the Nationals’ Joey Wiemer cranked a three-run home run into the left-field bleachers.</p><p>The Cubs answered with three homers, two by Alex Bregman, but were never able to overcome the early deficit. The Nationals never trailed in a 6-3 victory before 33,559 at Wrigley Field.</p><p>Imanaga gave up home runs by the barrelful last season, especially in the last two months.</p><p>In Imanaga’s last dozen starts of 2025, he was taken deep a staggering 20 times. During that stretch, he went 2-5 with a 5.17 ERA. This, after he missed a couple of months with a strained hamstring.</p><p>With Imanaga’s velocity frequently dipping below 90 mph — he averaged 90.5 mph last season — and his arm slot noticeably dropping, which also affected the effectiveness of his splitter, the Cubs declined to exercise their three-year, $57 million option on his deal.</p><p>For his part, Imanaga declined to exercise the $15 million player option he was holding. Two weeks later, however, he accepted the Cubs’ qualifying offer of $22.5 million, which the team had extended in hopes of receiving a compensatory draft choice as a parting gift if Imanaga elected to go.</p><p>Imanaga stayed put. A skeptic might say the Cubs were stuck with him, although if they had been that determined to let him go, they never would have made the qualifying offer.</p><p>Besides, Imanaga had pitched pretty well for them before he was hurt and the late-season collapse. Overall, he was 9-8 with a 3.73 ERA in 25 starts. And while his strikeout numbers were down (7.3 per nine innings after nine in 2024), there was reason for hope.</p><p>Manager Craig Counsell said he wasn’t looking for the old Imanaga to return. More like a Shota 2.0, as someone suggested.</p><p>‘‘We’re all a new version of ourselves,’’ Counsell said. ‘‘When you wake up the next day, you’re a new version of yourself.</p><p>‘‘So it’s more like a 2.0 than going backward because you’re always evolving.’’’</p><p>The problem came when Imanaga rolled out of bed Sunday and did something he rarely did in his first two seasons with the Cubs: He gave up a homer with at least two men on base. That happened only twice in each of the last two seasons.</p><p>‘‘Shota is going to give up home runs,’’ Counsell said. ‘‘It’s kind of the nature of the pitcher he is. But the three-run home runs, you have to prevent those because those are game-changing swings.</p><p>‘‘When they’re solo shots and you can limit the damage on them, it works. But the big swings like that, it’s hard to win games with three-run homers on the other side.’’</p><p>Except for walking the leadoff man in the sixth, which led to his departure and — ultimately — the Nationals’ fourth run, Imanaga pitched creditably after the first. He struck out seven and stranded Wiemer after a leadoff triple in the fourth.</p><p>‘‘Unfortunately, I gave up the three-run home run,’’’ he said. ‘‘But after that, I was telling myself, my body is feeling solid, I trust myself, I trust my stuff.’’</p><p>Winning the Cubs’ trust will be a work in progress.</p><p>‘‘The [home run] pitch was down, but .  . . it was a pitch I missed,’’ Imanaga said. ‘‘I need to be better at executing the pitch, location and zone.’’</p><h3>Suzuki update</h3><p>Outfielder Seiya Suzuki (sprained right knee) is progressing and eligible to come off the injured list Wednesday, but Counsell said it is more likely he will be activated on the Cubs' road trip, which begins Friday in Cleveland. Suzuki ran the bases and did some outfield drills before the game Sunday, and while he is close to running at full speed, Counsell said the team would like to see him sliding before giving him the green light to return.</p><h3>Bregman powers up</h3><p></p><p>Alex Bregman now has eight multihomer games in his career after hitting his first two as a Cub on Sunday.</p><p>"We need to clean it up a little bit,'' Bregman said of losing the first series. "We'll be better for it moving forward.''</p><p>His impressions of his first weekend in Chicago?</p><p>"It's awesome,'' he said. "Unbelievable atmosphere. The fans are incredible. So much history here, obviously so much to go out there and play day games here. My family is having a blast.''</p><h3>Notes</h3><p>Closer Daniel Palencia, still basking in the glow of Venezuela's thrilling victory against Team USA in the World Baseball Classic, got his first work of the season Sunday, giving up two hits in the ninth in a non-save situation. Palencia was spared a run when center fielder Pete Crow-Armstrong and shortstop Dansby Swanson executed a perfect relay to cut down Luis Garcia at the plate on a double by Daylen Lile.</p><p>• Veteran outfielder Michael Conforto, who made the club after signing in February on a non-roster basis, made his first start in right field. He went 0-for-2 with a walk while batting ninth.<br></p>
entries.author: Jeff Agrest

entries.title: Boy, 17, shot in South Chicago
entries.summary: <p>A teenage boy was hurt in a shooting in South Chicago Sunday night, police said.</p><p>The boy, 17, was on a sidewalk in the 8400 block of South Muskegon Avenue just before 7 p.m. when a car approached and someone inside fired shots, Chicago police said.</p><p>The boy was shot in the shoulder and taken to University of Chicago Medical Center, where he was listed in good condition.</p><p>No one is in custody and detectives are investigating.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Nico Hoerner officially becomes next Cub to cash in
entries.summary: <p>Second baseman Nico Hoerner’s mom and dad, both teachers who never pushed him to be a ballplayer, and his two sisters sat in the front row with his fianceé, Julia, who was presented roses by the Cubs.</p><p>At least a dozen teammates filled one side of the room, including locker mate Ian Happ, along with manager Craig Counsell.</p><p>Hoerner and Cubs president of baseball operations Jed Hoyer exchanged bouquets, with Hoyer saying that, from the time the Cubs drafted Hoerner 24th overall in 2018, they had identified him as a player who met their vision of what it was to be a Cub.</p><p>Negotiations that began in January met the Opening Day deadline Hoerner had set for a deal to be struck — or close enough: Hoyer said the sides completed negotiations during the game.</p><p>‘‘I thought the coolest thing was each scouting report said, ‘Our kind of player, needs to be a Cub, plays like a Cub,’ ’’ Hoyer said. ‘‘Every one of the scouting reports was consistent in this is the kind of player we want to have. . . .</p><p>‘‘That could not be more true. When I think of Nico as a player and a person, he’s so unbelievably consistent, and I think sometimes consistency can be underrated or unappreciated. . . . He’s been a top-20 player, by any estimation in the league, by being so unbelievably consistent.’’</p><p>It has rarely paid better to be a Cub. On Friday, the Cubs announced they had signed center fielder Pete Crow-Armstrong to a six-year, $115 million deal. After the game Sunday, in a second-floor conference room in the Cubs’ offices, they announced a six-year, $141 million deal for Hoerner.</p><p>Hoerner was eligible for free agency after the season and, as much as he expressed his love for playing in Chicago, acknowledged he was prepared to test the market if it came to that. It was important to him that he be valued the way he perceived he should.</p><p>So is this what the Cubs look like with the financial gloves off — big paydays for Crow-Armstrong and Hoerner, preceded by the five-year, $175 million free-agent signing of third baseman Alex Bregman?</p><p>Well, sure, but it requires some context: The Cubs have a lot of money potentially coming off the books after the season. They still have six players potentially eligible for free agency after the season: Happ, Seiya Suzuki, Shota Imanaga, Jameson Taillon, Matthew Boyd and Carson Kelly.</p><p>If you’re sitting in Hoyer’s chair, that clearly makes it easier to make the case to owner Tom Ricketts that, with such clean books, this is an ideal time to lock up the pieces you’ve identified as keys to sustaining success, especially with the next couple of free-agent classes lacking in star power.</p><p>‘‘Reaching free agency in our sport is a really big deal,’’ Hoerner said. ‘‘It takes time, a lot of help and fortune and opportunity. It’s not something to take lightly when that opportunity is close. But you also step back and take a look at what opportunity would be better, and I’m in a place right now, I don’t think there’s a better place to play in our sport right now.</p><p>‘‘We’ve got an incredible opportunity in front of us right now, and having a taste of the playoffs last year and seeing what that’s like, it’s a thing that gives me a lot of confidence.’’</p><p>Hoerner said he gave some thought to the looming labor situation, in which a lockout appears to be all but certain, but said it didn’t play much of a role in his decision. He noted that during the last lockout, most top-end free agents came pretty quickly to agreements with signing clubs.</p><p>Here is the breakdown of the Hoerner deals, as confirmed by a major-league source:</p><p><b>Nico Hoerner's contract:</b> </p><p>Six years, $141 million</p><p>$5 million signing bonus</p><p>$23 million in 2027-28</p><p>$22.5 million in 2029-32</p><p>$25 million deferred</p>
entries.author: Gordon Edes

entries.title: Illinois will face UConn in Final Four in rematch of Huskies' November victory
entries.summary: <p>Just when still-walking-on-air Illinois fans were sure the team’s Final Four opponent would be Duke, UConn staged an Elite Eight comeback Sunday that belongs on the short list of the greatest in <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports" target="_blank" >NCAA Tournament</a> history.</p><p>Facing the Blue Devils, the No. 1 seed in the East Region — not to mention everybody’s national player of the year, Cameron Boozer — the No. 2-seeded Huskies clawed back from 17 points down in the second half and won 73-72 after a steal by Silas Demary Jr. with :06 on the clock and a 35-foot three-pointer by freshman Braylon Mullins that splashed into the net with less than a half-second to go. It was their first lead since the game was 2-0.</p><p>That’s the steely-spined 33-5 opponent the 28-8 Illini will have to find a way to bull through Saturday (5:09 p.m., TBS, TNT, truTV) in Indianapolis if they’re going to reach the championship game <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/03/28/illinois-iowa-final-four-wagler-stirtz-underwood-mccollum-ncaa-tournament-elite-eight-march-madness" target="_blank" >for the first time since 2005</a> and win it all for the first time in their history.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/03/29/illinois-michigan-big-ten-final-four-championship-drought-michigan-state-spartans-mateen-cleaves-tom-izzo-2000" >Illinois, Michigan give Big Ten two shots at ending quarter-century-long title drought. Is this the year?</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/03/28/illinois-iowa-final-four-wagler-stirtz-underwood-mccollum-ncaa-tournament-elite-eight-march-madness" >Illinois Final Four-bound for first time since 2005 after beating Iowa in South Region final</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports/2026/03/27/illinois-iowa-rivalry-deon-thomas-bruce-pearl-elite-eight-ncaa-tourmament-march-madness" target="_blank" >Illinois vs. Iowa: a rivalry so Elite, it might as well be 1989</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>If the Illini are your team, you’ve probably seen enough of UConn to last you awhile longer than this.</p><p>Who can forget the beating for the ages UConn gave Illinois in the Elite Eight just two years ago? Three words — ‘‘30-point run’’ — covers the carnage.</p><p></p><p>This season, the Illini lost to the Huskies 74-61 at Madison Square Garden the day after Thanksgiving. Illini veteran Kylan Boswell had 25 points and nine rebounds in that game, but freshman Keaton Wagler was overmatched and then-starter Andrej Stojakovic brought nothing to the table, either. Wagler and Stojakovic, who’ve been so good in this tournament, scored only three points each. Also, the Huskies had the rebounding edge (43-38) against the tallest team in the country.</p><p></p><p>‘‘They’re very good,’’ coach Brad Underwood said after that game. ‘‘I hope we see them later down the road again because I think they have an opportunity to make a very deep run.’’</p><p></p><p>Good call, Coach. You nailed it.</p><p></p><p>Now Underwood gets what he wished for — another crack at the Huskies and at coach Dan Hurley, owner of two national titles in the last three years and the attitude of someone who has won three or four times that many.</p><p></p><p>‘‘We just believe we’re supposed to win this time of year,’’ Hurley said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">OH MY GOODNESS 😱<br><br>UCONN LEADSSSS UNBELIEVABLE <a href="https://twitter.com/hashtag/MarchMadness?src=hash&ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">#MarchMadness</a> <a href="https://t.co/IPX2JWiw0b">pic.twitter.com/IPX2JWiw0b</a></p>— NCAA March Madness (@MarchMadnessMBB) <a href="https://twitter.com/MarchMadnessMBB/status/2038396242200277362?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 29, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Illinois is in the Final Four for the sixth time and for the third since the tournament expanded to 32 teams in 1975. Only Houston has more appearances (seven) without winning a title. Only Purdue has more tournament victories (53) than Illinois’ 50 among schools that haven’t won it all.</p><p>‘‘I never doubted — and I don’t want to sound arrogant — I’ve never doubted us getting to a Final Four would happen,’’ Underwood said Saturday after beating Iowa. ‘‘I’ve thought we had other teams capable, but I also know how doggone hard it is to do it. For that I say, ‘Thank you. Thank you to everybody involved.’ ’’</p><p></p><p>The Illini have been celebrating with Super Soaker fights in their locker room after tournament victories.</p><p></p><p>‘‘You can never not celebrate winning,’’ Underwood said. ‘‘We work our asses off.’’</p><p></p><p>To get past UConn, they’re going to have to play them off.</p><p></p><p>‘‘I don’t want anybody to think that this is it,’’ Stojakovic said. ‘‘We didn’t get to the Final Four just to get there. We’re coming to win two more games.’’</p>
entries.author: Steve Greenberg

entries.title: Horoscope for Monday, March 30, 2026
entries.summary: <h2>Moon Alert</h2> <p>There are no restrictions to shopping or important decisions today. The moon is in Virgo.</p><h2>Aries (March 21-April 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Today, three planets are in your sign, giving you power, focus and purpose. Meanwhile, fair Venus is now in your money house, attracting money and favors to you and encouraging you to buy pretty things for yourself and loved ones.</p><h2>Taurus (April 20-May 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Today, fair Venus moves into your sign to stay for four weeks, encouraging peace, art, diplomacy and romance all around you. Not only will you find it easy to be charming with everyone, but you'll also be successful shopping for wardrobe treasures.</p><h2>Gemini (May 21-June 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> This is a friendly time for you because Venus is now traveling through your house of friendships. It's also an excellent time for you to talk to bosses, parents and authority figures because your ruler Mercury is at the top of your chart.</p><h2>Cancer (June 21-July 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Lucky you! Relations with groups and friends will be warm and rewarding in the next four weeks. For some of you, a casual friendship might turn into a flirtation or even a serious romance. Anything can happen while lucky Jupiter is in your sign.</p><h2>Leo (July 23-Aug. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> You make a marvelous impression on everyone in the next four weeks because fair Venus is at the top of your chart creating favorable circumstances for you and your business and professional life. Someone will ask for your artistic advice about something.</p><h2>Virgo (Aug. 23-Sept. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> You will long for opportunities to travel for pleasure in the next four weeks. (If a chance to do so comes your way, grab it.) Meanwhile, romance with someone "different" might present itself. Use the next four weeks to enjoy art exhibits, galleries, museums and concerts. Embrace beauty where you find it.</p><h2>Libra (Sept. 23-Oct. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Your ruler, Venus, will be sitting in your Eighth House for the next four weeks, igniting romance and pushing relationships into warmer affection and deeper passion. This same influence can attract money to you through your spouse, a business partner or even a bank. Ka-ching!</p><h2>Scorpio (Oct. 23-Nov. 21)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Fair Venus will be opposite your sign for the next four weeks, enhancing all your relationships — love affairs, relations with partners and co-workers, even your relationships with your enemies. Certainly, if you're in conflict with someone, this will be the ideal window to make peace. Timing is everything.</p><h2>Sagittarius (Nov. 22-Dec. 21)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> You are high-viz today, which means people will notice you more than usual. (In fact, some will know personal details about your private life.) Be aware of this. Meanwhile, relations with work colleagues will improve in the next four weeks. Note: You'll accomplish more through teamwork.</p><h2>Capricorn (Dec. 22-Jan. 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Do something to shake up your day a little. You want adventure and stimulation! Go someplace you've never been before. Explore interesting places and meet new people. Meanwhile, the next four weeks promise fun opportunities to socialize. Enjoy romance, activities with children and sports-related events.</p><h2>Aquarius (Jan. 20-Feb. 18)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Pleasant, peaceful moments will be your theme for the next four weeks, including enjoying quiet time at home. You might choose to entertain. You might also do something to redecorate or tweak your digs or make needed improvements. You don't have to be extravagant — just please yourself.</p><h2>Pisces (Feb. 19-March 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> As Venus changes signs today, it ushers in a four-week window of opportunities for you to have more appreciation of your daily surroundings. Look around you. See the world with fresh eyes. You might be surprised at the beauty you encounter on a daily basis. Who knew?</p><h2>If today is your birthday</h2><p>Guitarist, singer, songwriter Eric Clapton (1945) shares your birthday today. You are driven because you want to experience everything in the world. You are fearless and imaginative. This is a year of learning and teaching. Take time to renew your spiritual or religious beliefs. Explore philosophies that will bring you closer to the true meaning of your life.</p>
entries.author: Georgia Nicols

entries.title: State trooper among 2 hurt in crash involving squad car near Garfield Park
entries.summary: <p>A state trooper was among two people hurt in a crash involving an Illinois state police squad car early Monday near Garfield Park on the West Side. </p><p>Around 4:15 a.m., state troopers responded to a crash involving the injured trooper at West Jackson Boulevard and South Homan Avenue, state police said. </p><p>The trooper and the driver of the second vehicle were taken to area hospitals with unspecified injuries, officials said. </p><p>No further details were immediately available. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Why Connor Bedard's offensive growth with Blackhawks stems from him creating more space
entries.summary: <p>NEWARK, N.J. — At <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a> coach Jeff Blashill's introductory news conference last May, he mentioned how Connor Bedard creating more space for himself in the offensive zone could help him take the next step.</p><p>His shooting, passing and stick-handling skills have always been elite, but without space, they're useless. And with opposing defenses keying on him — especially since his Hawks linemates have never posed equivalent threats — he will never have space unless he can create it.</p><p>Almost a year later, Blashill's point has proven prescient. Bedard has taken the next step toward superstardom this season, and that's largely because he has found ways to separate himself from opponents' sticks and bodies.</p><p>The 20-year-old center had already reached the 30-goal threshold for the first time and set a career high with 68 points, even though he missed 13 games and was hampered for an additional 13 with a shoulder injury. </p><p>His 1.11 points-per-game rate ranks 20th in the NHL.</p><p>Blashill notes that players can create more space in three ways: increased speed, increased strength and increased smarts.</p><p>Bedard's improvement in speed has been the most obvious and quantifiable. It was the No. 1 focus of his offseason training last summer, and it became evident immediately during training camp that his work had paid off. </p><p>He has recorded 174 speed bursts faster than 20 mph this season, up from 70 last season and 102 as a rookie (in fewer games, no less). He equaled his fastest recorded speed of 22.36 mph on a spirited forecheck <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/03/26/blackhawks-flyers-poor-defense-louis-crevier-sacha-boisvert" target="_blank" >Thursday against the Flyers</a>.</p><p>As Blashill puts it, "He's a fast player now." It's as simple as that.</p><p>On the strength front, Bedard has improved a little bit, but he was already strong as an 18-year-old. The bigger difference is his balance on his skates, which is connected to his skating improvement but might look like a strength improvement.</p><p>His goal <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/03/19/blackhawks-wild-connor-bedard-holding-2-0-lead" target="_blank" >March 19 in Minnesota</a>, for example, demonstrated both improvements. After stealing the puck from Wild veteran Marcus Johansson at the defensive blue line, he accelerated for a breakaway, then used his body positioning and skating edge-work to prevent Johansson from catching him from behind.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">CONNOR BEDARD STEALS THE PUCK AND FINDS THE BACK OF THE NET 🗣️ <a href="https://t.co/DkhqCJ6sse">pic.twitter.com/DkhqCJ6sse</a></p>— Sportsnet (@Sportsnet) <a href="https://twitter.com/Sportsnet/status/2034798074946539540?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 20, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Board battles have been another focus for Bedard. By winning more of those, he spends more time possessing the puck and less time tangled up with other players.</p><p>"I'm obviously not the biggest guy in the world, but you can use your strength and stability," Bedard said Friday. "I'm not going to win every battle, but [I've learned] how to turn into stuff. Maybe [I'm] not getting pinned up against the wall as much. I've definitely gotten better in that sense."</p><p>But it's increased smarts — or more accurately, increased knowledge of how to use his inherent smarts — that has made the largest difference.</p><p>"My biggest strength is my brain," Bedard said. "That's where I think I can maybe out-think people.</p><p>Added Blashill: "He's a really, really smart player. [It's] probably one of the things he's underrated with. People talk about his shot, but his game is so much more than that. [He's an] extraordinarily smart offensive player."</p><p>Bedard's goal Thursday demonstrated that well. As the Hawks cycled the puck down low in the offensive zone, he hung back near the blue line for several seconds — long enough to be mostly forgotten by the Flyers. </p><p>Then he anticipated a gap in coverage opening off to the side of the goal, and he began racing down toward it before it actually opened. Flyers captain Sean Couturier was caught flat-footed, so Bedard had acres of room to bury a well-placed pass from Anton Frondell. </p><p>"[I've learned] when to do stuff, when not to, and [how to find] little soft areas," Bedard said. "I'm probably a little more patient getting into spots."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">ANTON FRONDELL TO CONNOR BEDARD 🔥 <br><br>THE FUTURE OF THE HAWKS IS HERE! <a href="https://t.co/TspVpmEpwq">pic.twitter.com/TspVpmEpwq</a></p>— NHL (@NHL) <a href="https://twitter.com/NHL/status/2037327833593262378?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 27, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>With that extra patience, Bedard isn't charging as frequently into areas that turn out to be too tight, which often leads to turnovers. He's choosier about picking his spots, then fully committing once he makes the decision.</p><p>Even then, the spaces are usually still tight and rapidly tightening. That's just how the NHL works. </p><p>But as long as there's a sliver of room, his hands are good enough to maneuver the puck through, and his release is deceptive enough to avoid stick-checks. His ability to shoot accurately from almost underneath his body helps, too.</p><p>That coiled-snake-waiting-to-strike approach sounds like Lightning star Nikita Kucherov, one of Bedard's most influential models. In fact, Bedard recently read a story in The Athletic that discussed how Kucherov watches video clips so religiously that he has memorized the strengths and weaknesses of every opposing defenseman.</p><p>"I'm nowhere near the level of that," Bedard said, chuckling. "But I think [for] a lot of offensive guys — and myself — the brain is probably the biggest thing."</p><p>Bedard wasn't at his best throughout the Hawks' New York-area road trip, and he admitted to it. He still has growth to do, especially defensively, and the team he leads still has <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/03/29/blackhawks-devils-spencer-knight-frank-nazar-defensive-issues" target="_blank" >tons of growth to do</a>.</p><p>Nevertheless, he has squashed any concerns about his long-term potential to be a franchise cornerstone with such an impressive third NHL season.</p><p>"[I'll] just keep getting better and take it day-by-day," he said.</p>
entries.author: Ben Pope

entries.title: Small, independent landlords will need support if city mandates rental inspections
entries.summary: <p>The city of Chicago’s move toward <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.councilmatic.org/legislation/o2025-0019991/" target="_blank" >mandatory pre-rental inspections</a> raises an obvious and unanswered question: Who will be expected to bear the cost?</p><p>If history is any guide, the answer will likely be small, independent landlords — the very people already navigating rising property taxes, insurance premiums, maintenance costs and increasingly complex regulatory burdens. This would be yet another in a long series of incremental pressures — the “thousand paper cuts”— that collectively make it harder to provide affordable housing at the neighborhood level.</p><p>At the <a class="Link" href="https://www.thecha.org/manage-properties-i-own/inspections" target="_blank" >Chicago Housing Authority</a>, inspections are funded by the agency itself. In those cases, the public entity requiring compliance also assumes the financial responsibility. Here, the city appears poised to mandate compliance while shifting the cost entirely onto property owners.</p><p>Let’s be candid: Policies like this don’t exist in a vacuum. When you increase the cost of operating small rental properties, those costs are inevitably passed along, whether through higher rents, reduced investment in maintenance or landlords exiting the market altogether. None of those outcomes benefits tenants or neighborhoods.</p><p>If the city insists on implementing a pre-rental inspection regime, it should at a minimum:<br></p><ol class="rte2-style-ol" id="rte-cdf267a1-287b-11f1-8ac2-5bdd3a82ee6b" start="1"><li>Clearly disclose all associated costs and fees</li><li>Provide subsidies or waivers for small landlords</li><li>Ensure the program is about safety and habitability — not revenue generation.</li></ol><p>Absent those safeguards, this initiative could look less like sound housing policy and more like a thinly veiled revenue grab — one that risks doing more harm than good in the very communities it claims to protect.</p><p><i>Edward G. Irvin, landlord, Auburn Gresham</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-400000" name="module-400000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>Give us your take</h3><br>Send letters to the editor to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. To be considered for publication, letters must include your full name, your neighborhood or hometown and a phone number for verification purposes. Letters should be a maximum of approximately 375 words.</div> </div><p></p><h3>Pungent phrases</h3><p>Yeah, they said it.</p><p>"It’s hypocritical to say that these attacks harmed (Iranian) women and children when those women and children — the young girls that you reference — would live a life in a barbaric, unequal society behind a burqa, with no ability to make career choices" — <a class="Link" href="https://www.uscannenbergmedia.com/2026/03/09/international-womens-day-takes-on-new-significance-after-bombing-of-iranian-girls-school/" target="_blank" >Matt Schlapp</a>, chairman, American Conservative Union.</p><p>"I think it's worth to have a cost of, unfortunately, some gun deaths every single year so that we can have the Second Amendment to protect our other God-given rights." — <a class="Link" href="https://www.newsweek.com/charlie-kirk-says-gun-deaths-worth-it-2nd-amendment-1793113" target="_blank" >Charlie Kirk</a>.</p><p>"I could stand in the middle of Fifth Avenue and shoot somebody, and I wouldn't lose any voters, OK?" — <a class="Link" href="https://www.npr.org/sections/thetwo-way/2016/01/23/464129029/donald-trump-i-could-shoot-somebody-and-i-wouldnt-lose-any-voters" target="_blank" >Donald Trump</a>.</p><p>Remember President Richard Nixon’s <a class="Link" href="https://www.history.com/this-day-in-history/november-17/nixon-insists-that-he-is-not-a-crook" target="_blank" >“I’m not a crook”</a> quote? Such an innocent time.</p><p><i>Jim Newton, Itasca</i></p><h3>Privatize TSA tasks</h3><p>Amid all the teeth-gnashing, hand-wringing and political preening about Department of Homeland Security funding and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/travel/2026/03/24/what-we-know-and-dont-know-about-tsa-wait-times-at-ohare-and-midway-airports" >Transportation Security Administration</a> issues, one very important observation goes virtually without notice. The 20 U.S. airports that employ private security (e.g. <a class="Link" href="https://www.cnn.com/2026/03/21/us/airports-without-tsa#:~:text=At%2020%20airports%20in%20the,uses%20contractors%20at%20the%20checkpoints." target="_blank" >San Francisco, Kansas City and Orlando</a>) have continued to function rather nicely during the partial government shutdown. Indeed, they were more effectively and efficiently run before the current TSA kerfuffle. That should come as no surprise, as private industry almost always outperforms the government.</p><p>If there is any good to come out of the current attention to airport security, the best thing would be to privatize that function.</p><p><i>William P. Gottschalk, Lake Forest</i></p><h3>No will to fix lapses</h3><p>Joe Biden may have let <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/24/chicago-crime-trump-pritzker-bovino-immigration-midway-blitz-deport-immigration" target="_blank" >Jose G. Medina</a>, Sheridan Gorman's alleged murderer, into the country, which was wrong. But what is currently being done to correct the problem? The Trump administration has had more than a year and millions of taxpayers' dollars to stop Medina and maybe could have.</p><p>Instead, there have been too many missteps along the way, and now Department of Homeland Security agents are in airports trying to learn Transportation Security Administration jobs. All of us want these criminals to be stopped, but is anything productive really being done?</p><p><i>Tom DeDore, Garfield Ridge</i></p><h3>Selective outrage</h3><p>President Donald Trump loves to throw shade at Democrats when people who shouldn't be in the country cause damage, like the undocumented migrant who allegedly gunned down Loyola University student<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/22/ice-detainer-loyola-student-killing-sheridan-gorman" > Sheridan Gorman</a>.</p><p>But where is the outrage when someone pardoned by Trump causes great harm?</p><p>One example: Andrew Paul Johnson was convicted in August 2024 and sentenced to a year in prison for his role in the Jan. 6 “disturbance” at the U.S. Capitol. On Jan. 20, 2025, Trump <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/metro-state/2025/01/20/trumps-pardons-for-jan-6-riot-helps-more-than-50-defendants-from-illinois" >pardoned Johnson as well as everyone</a> else involved in that riot, no matter how violently they behaved.</p><p>On March 5, Johnson was convicted of two counts of child molestation, at least some of which occurred after he was released from prison, and <a class="Link" href="https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2026/mar/05/capitol-rioter-life-sentence-molesting-children" target="_blank" >sentenced to life if prison</a>.</p><p>Does Trump feel any guilt or responsibility for the subsequent behavior of people he released back into society, or does his kind of outrage only apply to the other side?</p><p><i>Kevin Coughlin, Evanston</i></p><h3><b>Midterms will tell us a lot</b></h3><p>If there is voter's remorse for electing Donald Trump, we're not going to hear it from those voters. With all the political divisiveness in our country right now, who would admit to being on the losing end of his or her candidate selection?</p><p>We will find out in November's midterm elections.</p><p><i>John Petersen, Belmont Heights</i></p><h3>Limit mail-in ballot use </h3><p>Debating the constitutionality of accepting late <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/live/supreme-court-mail-in-ballots" target="_blank" >mail-in ballots</a> is a diversion from the real issue: whether mail-in voting should be accepted at all.</p><p>Why do we have polling places in the first place?</p><p>When people vote in person, we know who is voting and we know they are voting in secret, free from outside influences.</p><p>When people vote at home, we have no idea who is filling out the ballot or whether they were alone when they did.</p><p>Voting in person is a minimum safeguard for our elections. Mail-in voting may be allowed, perhaps only in the rarest of circumstances.</p><p><i>Larry Craig, Wilmette</i></p>
entries.author: Letters to the Editor

entries.title: Nurses are an afterthought in health care debate, and that's deadly
entries.summary: <p>When President Donald Trump takes aim at costs imposed on Americans by “big health insurance companies,” he proposes stopping payments to insurers and instead giving the money directly to Americans so they can purchase care themselves “at a much lower cost.”</p><p>What is largely absent from the conversation surrounding rising prices is a serious examination of the role hospitals play in driving up health care costs — and, ultimately, what Americans pay every year.</p><p>Politically, this omission makes sense. Americans are furious about premiums, deductibles, out-of-pocket costs and the constant fear coverage could hinge on a single phone call. Insurers are easy targets. They are visible, bureaucratic and widely disliked.</p><p>But to be serious about lowering costs requires zooming out.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-a20000" name="module-a20000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Commentary bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Commentary</h2></div> </div><p><a class="Link" href="https://www.beckershospitalreview.com/hr/7-healthcare-strikes-in-2026/" target="_blank" >For much of 2026</a>, thousands of registered nurses across the country have stood on picket lines, warning through collective action that something inside hospital systems was breaking. They have raised concerns about unsafe staffing levels, unsustainable patient loads and business models that treat labor as a line item to be squeezed rather than the backbone of patient care.</p><p>Those warnings did not begin with a strike and did not end when nurses returned to work.</p><p>There is an old saying in health care: Nurses’ working conditions are patients’ healing conditions. That is not poetic. It is policy.</p><p>If hospital services account for nearly a third of all U.S. health spending — roughly $1.6 trillion in 2024, according to the <a class="Link" href="https://www.healthsystemtracker.org/chart-collection/u-s-spending-healthcare-changed-time/#Total%20national%20health%20expenditures,%201970-2024" target="_blank" >Kaiser Family Foundation</a> — any serious conversation about affordability must include what is happening inside hospital systems.</p><p>And here is what often gets overlooked: Nurses’ work rarely appears clearly on a hospital bill. Patients see the facility charge, the imaging charge and the pharmacy line item.</p><p>What they do not see are the hours of monitoring that prevent cardiac events, the medication double-check that stops dosing errors or the early intervention that prevents a costly intensive care unit admission.</p><p>The most essential labor in health care is <a class="Link" href="https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5887158/#:~:text=Nurses%20are%20an%20anomaly%20in,Welton%20&%20Harris%2C%202007)." target="_blank" >invisible on an invoice</a>.</p><h3>Patients pay for staff cuts</h3><p>When hospital systems cut staffing to improve margins, the immediate savings may look efficient on paper. But thin staffing often means longer stays, higher complication rates, avoidable readmissions and burnout-driven turnover, all of which are enormously expensive.</p><p>Labor decisions upstream drive costs downstream.</p><p>Meanwhile, <a class="Link" href="https://www.gao.gov/blog/health-care-becoming-more-consolidated-including-physicians.-what-effect-it-having" target="_blank" >hospital consolidation has accelerated dramatically</a> over the past two decades. Large systems have acquired competitors, strengthened their negotiating leverage and expanded their regional dominance. Research consistently shows as hospital competition declines, prices for privately insured patients rise — often without measurable improvements in quality.</p><p>That trend should prompt investigations in state capitols and congressional hearing rooms alike. Instead, much of the political focus remains fixed on insurers and on rerouting payment streams.</p><p>However, health care inflation is not just about who writes the check. It is about who sets the price.</p><p>For-profit and nonprofit hospital systems wield enormous power in that equation. <a class="Link" href="https://www.fiercehealthcare.com/special-reports/heres-what-ceos-and-cfos-public-profit-health-systems-made-2024#:~:text=Here's%20what%20made%20up%20the%20CEO's%20$23.8%20million%20in%20pay,Source%20Sans%20Pro" target="_blank" >Executive compensation packages</a> can exceed $10 million at public for-profit hospitals. At the same time, frontline employees continue to report staffing pressures that stretch patient safety.</p><p>This is why the voices of registered nurses matter so deeply in this debate. Not only are they among the most trusted professionals in the country, but their vantage point is operational rather than ideological. They see where resources are abundant and where they are scarce. They see how administrative expansion compares with bedside staffing and how consolidation reshapes care delivery in real time.</p><p>They understand something policymakers often overlook: You cannot cut your way to high-quality, low-cost care by shrinking the workforce that actually delivers it.</p><p>If Washington truly wants to lower health care costs, the conversation must move beyond slogans about insurers and toward structural reform that includes confronting consolidation, enforcing price transparency, establishing safe staffing standards and redesigning incentives to reward outcomes rather than volume.</p><p>An honest examination of affordability requires <a class="Link" href="https://www.pbs.org/newshour/show/why-patients-are-getting-hit-with-surprise-hospital-fees-for-routine-medical-care" target="_blank" >scrutiny of hospital cost structures</a> and the market power that shapes them, because these costs are being passed on to patients. Until that happens, Americans will continue to feel the strain not just in their premiums but in the quality and safety of the care they receive.</p><p>This is the conversation the Trump administration should be having with the American people.</p><p><i>Richard Fowler, who grew up in Evanston, is a journalist and Forbes contributor who reports on health care costs, economic policy and their impact on working families. He is a Fox News contributor and adjunct professor at Georgetown University.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-690000" name="module-690000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Richard Fowler

entries.title: Dear Abby: Sister-in-law bitter when couple visit her ex
entries.summary: <p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> My sister-in-law "Nancy" and my wife have a strained relationship, but they love each other and talk often. Every so often, Nancy becomes abusive with my wife. The ire stems from a messy divorce Nancy went through 15 years ago.</p><p>We have traveled to see Nancy's son (our nephew) who lives with her ex, "Jim." While we are there, we see them both and enjoy some quality time together. This aggravates Nancy, who feels that because (in her opinion) Jim was solely responsible for the divorce, we are disrespecting her by visiting him. I think she should understand that we developed a relationship with him during their marriage.</p><p>We love Nancy, but we feel we are entitled to maintain the relationship with her ex. Are we wrong? Must we choose a side since she is so hateful toward him? — <i>WRONG SIDE IN TEXAS</i></p><p><b>DEAR WRONG SIDE:</b> Nancy is hurt and bitter that Jim left her, as well as possessive of you and her sister. Time has not mellowed her. You are not wrong to maintain a relationship with your former brother-in-law. As adults, you and your wife are entitled to have a relationship with anyone you wish. (It is also understandable that you would want to maintain a relationship with your nephew.) That said, however, it would seem prudent for the two of you to disclose less to Nancy about your travels because she is so sensitive and emotional about it.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> My cousin's son "Troy" is being married in eight months. I just received the "save the date," and I'm trying to decide whether to attend. The wedding is out of state, requiring travel and a hotel. Troy and I haven't spoken in years.</p><p>He has never shown an interest in getting to know me. The last communication I had with him was a thank-you note for his high school graduation gift eight years ago. Once, when Troy, his brother and his mother were supposed to spend a day or two visiting me while on vacation, they decided at the last minute to visit other relatives in California. And last year, when the family was supposed to come for Thanksgiving, they rented an Airbnb close to my house, and then everyone made plans to do things without me. Needless to say, I was surprised to receive his "save the date."</p><p>Must I attend? Should I attend? Should I send a gift, or simply convey my best wishes for a happy marriage, like I would to any other stranger or acquaintance? — <i>BAFFLED IN ARIZONA</i></p><p><b>DEAR BAFFLED:</b> Because relations with this branch of the family are so distant, I don't think you need to go to the expense of traveling to be there. However, the polite thing to do to maintain some sort of family connection would be to send a gift to the happy couple, along with a card conveying your good wishes. (My intuition tells me you likely won't receive an acknowledgement for your generosity, so don't be disappointed.)</p><p><i>Dear Abby is written by Abigail Van Buren, also known as Jeanne Phillips, and was founded by her mother, Pauline Phillips. Contact Dear Abby at www.DearAbby.com or P.O. Box 69440, Los Angeles, CA 90069.</i></p><p>To receive a collection of Abby's most memorable — and most frequently requested — poems and essays, send your name and mailing address, plus check or money order for $8 (U.S. funds) to: Dear Abby — Keepers Booklet, P.O. Box 446, Kings Mills, OH 45034-0446. (Shipping and handling are included in the price.)</p>
entries.author: Abigail Van Buren

entries.title: 2026 started as a renaissance year for composer Florence Price. Then came controversy
entries.summary: <p>2026 looked to be a renaissance year for the composer <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/curious-city/2022/07/21/who-was-florence-price" target="_blank" >Florence Price,</a> a Black woman who made music in Chicago that is now being championed by the classical music world more than 70 years after her death.</p><p>Minnesota Opera premiered a <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/culture-the-arts/classical/2026/02/05/florence-price-chicago-composer-minnesota-opera-arts-immigration-black-history-month" target="_blank" ><u>new work</u></a> in February about her life, and Cambridge University Press just released a scholarly companion to her music. Concerts featuring her compositions are scheduled everywhere from Baltimore to New York City this year.</p><p>But a New Year’s Concert in Vienna featuring her music — one of the most lauded events of the classical world in one of the genre’s most lauded venues, the Musikverein — has kicked up controversy for, arguably, not<i> </i>featuring her music at all.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-dc0000" name="image-dc0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Florence Price surged to prominence when a tranche of previously unknown scores were recovered from her summer home outside Kankakee, Illinois in 2009.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Special collections, University of Arkansas Library </p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Her 1939 piece “Rainbow Waltz,” originally written for piano, was arranged for orchestra and conducted by Yannick Nézet-Séguin in the performance. But the piece that was played that day was so heavily stylized and reharmonized that online commentators erupted in criticism that has not <a class="Link" href="https://www.inquirer.com/arts/florence-price-yannick-nezet-seguin-philadelphia-orchestra-20260321.html" target="_blank" ><u>ebbed</u></a> in the months since <a class="Link" href="https://www.theguardian.com/music/2026/mar/19/vienna-philharmonic-re-orchestration-florence-price" target="_blank" ><u>the concert.</u></a></p><p>Scholar Alexandra Kori Hill, who<b> </b>co-edited the new <a class="Link" href="https://www.cambridge.org/core/books/cambridge-companion-to-florence-b-price/3D58F23D2F563AD96D49382F9013627B" target="_blank" ><u>Cambridge companion</u></a> on Price’s music, describes “Rainbow Waltz” as rooted in 20th-century “Afro-American pastoralism.” That atmosphere, she says, is lost in the arrangement played in Vienna.</p><p>“In the classical tradition, there’s an expectation of performing the original of something, particularly with arrangements of music by a person whom we’re still starting to understand stylistically,” Hill said. “It becomes even more urgent when it is music by a person that comes from an underrepresented or systemically marginalized group of people.”</p><p>Price biographer Douglas Shadle says Price’s life was full of ambivalent victories. Her <a class="Link" href="https://cso.org/experience/article/8636/125-moments-072-prices-symphony-in-e-minor" target="_blank" ><u>1933 Chicago Symphony debut</u></a> is often hailed as a breakthrough for her and <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/classical-music/2026/02/27/margaret-bonds-composer-gravestone-northwestern-university" target="_blank" ><u>Margaret Bonds</u></a>, another Black Chicago composer who soloed on the same program. Less mentioned is the overture by John Powell, an avowed white supremacist, which opened the program.</p><p>“It feels like a very Price situation,” Shadle said of the New Year’s episode. “The positive, again, has this strange counterweight to it.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" Collections, University of Arkansas Libraries</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>A rearranged “Rainbow Waltz” </b></h3><p>Born in Little Rock, Ark. and a longtime resident of Chicago’s South Side, Price surged to prominence when a tranche of previously unknown scores were <a class="Link" href="https://www.ualrpublicradio.org/local-regional-news/2018-05-04/after-lost-scores-are-found-in-abandoned-house-musicians-give-life-to-florence-prices-music" target="_blank" ><u>recovered</u></a> from her summer home outside Kankakee, Illinois in 2009.</p><p>Most of those rediscovered works were not registered with the United States Copyright Office prior to their discovery, meaning many — including “Rainbow Waltz" — are now public domain.</p><p>Price never heard her music performed abroad. She was planning a trip to Europe the year she died, in 1953. Posthumously, she became the first composer of color and, indeed, the first non-European composer ever featured in the Vienna Philharmonic’s New Year’s Concert, one of the biggest events in classical music.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-ce0000" name="image-ce0000"></a> <picture
