data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b976673/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5817x3265+0+241/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9f%2F0d%2F39364fc94db08eaaa3bfa030b767%2Fap20001466506035.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The Vienna Philharmonic Orchestra, pictured here in January 2020, performs a traditional New Year’s concert at the golden hall of Vienna’s Musikverein, in Vienna, Austria, each year. The 2026 concert featured a controversial arrangement of Price’s music. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ronald Zak/AP</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The concert reaches around 50 million viewers in more than 150 countries every year, via TV broadcasts, public radio and online streams.</p><p>This isn’t your average classical concert. Since the New Year’s Concert began in 1939 — as a Nazi-era fundraiser, as the Vienna Philharmonic has <a class="Link" href="https://www.wienerphilharmoniker.at/en/newyearsconcert/tradition-and-history" target="_blank" ><u>more openly acknowledged</u></a> in recent years — waltzes and other Viennese light music have been the evening fare. <a class="Link" href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=e7Yd-Jn1juc&list=RDe7Yd-Jn1juc&start_radio=1" target="_blank" ><u>“The Blue Danube,”</u></a> by “waltz king” Johann Strauss Jr. (1825–1899), is one such famous staple. So is the clapalong <a class="Link" href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ztSI9DxsLyc&list=RDztSI9DxsLyc&start_radio=1" target="_blank" ><u>“Radetzky March”</u></a> by the composer’s father, Johann Strauss Sr. (1804–1849). Besides Price’s “Rainbow Waltz,” this year’s program featured works by exclusively Austrian composers, including both Strausses.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="200" height="150" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/rU5Id8LPDuQ?feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen title="Rainbow Waltz"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p>Both Price’s original and the Philharmonic’s version — by its favored arranger, Wolfgang Dörner — are in the same key: D-flat major, with a shared excursion to G-flat major. But as online commentators soon noted, what the Vienna Philharmonic performed was a world apart from Price’s “Rainbow Waltz."</p><p>Price’s version is introspective, with harmonic suspensions and bluesy modulations giving it a nostalgic twinge. By contrast, <a class="Link" href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Oz7hmV_boGU&list=RDOz7hmV_boGU&start_radio=1" target="_blank" ><u>Dörner’s arrangement</u></a> — the score to which the Vienna Philharmonic provided, upon request — turned the piece’s overall mood triumphant. He heavily stylized the main melody and reharmonized it so it resembled a textbook Viennese waltz. </p><p>The structure was different, too: An original introduction was added, as was a concluding coda that ratchets the key of the main melody up a half-step, to D major. (This is a popular trick in pop music, too: think of the middle of Whitney Houston’s <a class="Link" href="https://youtu.be/eH3giaIzONA?si=fWbRFuH-NG8L9uDw&t=210" target="_blank" ><u>“I Wanna Dance with Somebody.”</u></a>)</p><p>After hearing “Rainbow Waltz” on Austrian television<b>,</b> among those who joined <a class="Link" href="https://katherineneedlemanoboist.substack.com/p/a-musical-mystery" target="_blank" ><u>a chorus</u></a> <a class="Link" href="https://cooperm55.wixsite.com/jmc3/post/the-sincerest-form-of-insult" target="_blank" ><u>of prominent critical voices</u></a> was Johannes Glück, a Vienna-based musical theater composer and actor. At first, he didn’t know who Florence Price was. But when he sought out more information about “Rainbow Waltz” the following day, the music he found didn’t resemble what he’d heard.</p><p>“Whenever I have a feeling the work of a fellow composer is treated with disrespect, it makes me angry,” Glück said. “I feel this kind of solidarity.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="200" height="150" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/Oz7hmV_boGU?feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen title="Rainbow Waltz (Arr. for Orchestra by Wolfgang Dörner)"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p>In a video call from Vienna, Vienna Philharmonic chair and longtime violinist Daniel Froschauer, said he first learned about Price when Riccardo Muti and the CSO toured her Symphony No. 3 at the Musikverein in 2024.</p><p>He told WBEZ he was “surprised” by the backlash about the Dörner’s arrangement, particularly the accusation that it was meant to fool audiences. He claimed he specifically requested that Dörner “have in mind the sound of Johann Strauss Vater [Sr.] and the <i>ländler</i>” — another triple-time dance — “of Schubert.” From there, the piece was expanded so that it fit the conventional Viennese waltz formula: an introduction, a series of waltzes, then ending with an exuberant coda.</p><p>In other words, the discrepancy listeners noted was intentional.</p><p>“[‘Rainbow Waltz’] was written in 1939, and you will not get an exact sound of an 1800, 1900 waltz. But that's the setting of the New Year’s Concert,” Froschauer said. “The thought of having [Price] with us was more important than to say, ‘No, we can't use it, because there is no introduction.’”</p><p>This was not the first time Price’s music had been performed in Vienna. American bass-baritone Ryan Speedo Green included two of her songs on a 2015 recital at the Musikverein, the storied venue that also hosts the New Year’s Concert.</p><p>Mississippi-born, Chicago-educated conductor <a class="Link" href="https://www.williamgarfieldwalker.com/" target="_blank" ><u>William Garfield Walker</u></a> has also become an ambassador for Price’s music in Europe. His own <a class="Link" href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=t1OoLYJytc4" target="_blank" ><u>string orchestra arrangement</u></a> of her organ piece, “Adoration,” was live-streamed in 2020 as part of a G20 economic forum.</p><p>“After the first reading, the musicians fall in love with the music, and audiences have really enjoyed her works, as well,” said Walker, also a four-time award recipient from the Evanston-based Solti Foundation U.S.</p><p>But after the most widely viewed orchestral concert in the world put Price’s name — if not strictly her music — in front of countless audiences, some streams of Price recordings indeed went up, according to Walker, the conductor, and Michael Clark, a pianist who released a <a class="Link" href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rU5Id8LPDuQ&list=RDrU5Id8LPDuQ&start_radio=1" target="_blank" ><u>studio recording</u></a> of “Rainbow Waltz” in 2024.</p><p>Through a representative, Nézet-Séguin declined an interview and follow-up questions. But in a written statement, the conductor touted his experience conducting Price’s music and said the arrangement intended to “introduce her music to a wider audience.”</p><p>“Different arrangements of her ‘Rainbow Waltz’ allow her music to reach different audiences and contexts: Wolfgang Dörner’s arrangement highlighted connections to the Viennese waltz tradition, and Valerie Coleman’s emphasizes an American sonority,” he wrote. “My hope is that these arrangements continue to promote the life and work of Price and bring her genius to audiences worldwide.”</p><p>Dörner also declined to comment for this story.</p><h3><b>Lessons learned? </b></h3><h3></h3><p>A musical “arrangement” can refer to vastly different treatments. Sometimes they match originals bar-to-bar. Other times, they are shuffled about, or extra stitching is added to piece them together.</p><p>And the same work can transform hugely in the hands of different arrangers. For example, Philadelphia audiences will hear another perspective on “Rainbow Waltz” in June, when Nézet-Séguin conducts a new arrangement of the work by award-winning composer and flutist <a class="Link" href="https://www.valeriecoleman.com/" target="_blank" ><u>Valerie Coleman</u></a>.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-hangRight> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-3d0000" name="image-3d0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6cf5c40/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1272x714+0+504/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F49%2F12%2F1faa6c984570bc7d88d480d7f540%2F460a6acce92e5562f281576b9f0f6b08.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0cd6a35/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1272x714+0+504/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F49%2F12%2F1faa6c984570bc7d88d480d7f540%2F460a6acce92e5562f281576b9f0f6b08.png 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8668cad/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1272x714+0+504/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F49%2F12%2F1faa6c984570bc7d88d480d7f540%2F460a6acce92e5562f281576b9f0f6b08.png" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="460a6acce92e5562f281576b9f0f6b08.png" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8668cad/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1272x714+0+504/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F49%2F12%2F1faa6c984570bc7d88d480d7f540%2F460a6acce92e5562f281576b9f0f6b08.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/708c369/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1272x714+0+504/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F49%2F12%2F1faa6c984570bc7d88d480d7f540%2F460a6acce92e5562f281576b9f0f6b08.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8668cad/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1272x714+0+504/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F49%2F12%2F1faa6c984570bc7d88d480d7f540%2F460a6acce92e5562f281576b9f0f6b08.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Rediscovered after her death, many of Florence Price’s works were not registered with the United States Copyright Office. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Special collections, University of Arkansas Library</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Price’s work was arranged in her own lifetime, too — but not always in ways she approved or appreciated. A <a class="Link" href="https://digitalcollections.uark.edu/digital/collection/p17212coll3/id/0/rec/50" target="_blank" ><u>partial, undated complaint letter</u></a> recovered from her summer home blasts a big-band arrangement that she felt wasn’t up to snuff.</p><p>“That really annoyed her,” said Samantha Ege, a scholar and pianist who co-edited the Cambridge companion with Hill. “We have her perspective on it, and we understand that this was something she encountered in her own time.”</p><p>Even the Strausses, whose music is at the heart of Vienna’s New Year’s Concert, may not be happy with how their music is being presented, were they alive today. Their descendant Eduard Strauss — who runs a <a class="Link" href="https://www.johann-strauss.at/en/" target="_blank" ><u>research institute</u></a> dedicated to his family’s music in Vienna — certainly isn’t. He says the New Year’s concert employs a far larger orchestra, and he objects to crowd-pleasing enhancements of some of his forebears’ music — like the bombastic percussion in the “Radetzky March.”</p><p>“I'm not invited, and I don't go there,” said Strauss of the New Year’s Concerts, which he says he hasn’t attended in decades. “I'm a purist. I want to have the music heard as it was composed.”</p><p>Froschauer, the Vienna Philharmonic chair, is conflicted over whether, in hindsight, the Philharmonic ought to have labeled Dörner’s creation as something other than an “arrangement.” </p><p>“Whatever we do seems to be at the center of attention. So, I'm just glad that Florence Price got to be the center of attention,” Froschauer said. “What I learned from this experience is to be, maybe, a little bit more careful.”</p><p>Today, Price has more advocates than ever. But as with any explosion of interest in a figure, Ege, the Price scholar and pianist, urges a deeper engagement from musicians and online commentators alike.</p><p>"I think that's how we ensure Price's legacy: by really spending time with her,” she says.<br></p>
entries.author: Hannah Edgar | WBEZ

entries.title: Top Mormon leader D. Todd Christofferson knew around 2020 that his brother was an alleged child molester
entries.summary: <p>A top Mormon church leader who has said he learned in or about 2020 that his brother may have molested children many years earlier — possibly involving a congregation in the Chicago region — didn’t notify police or child welfare authorities.</p><p>With the accusers grown, Mormon leader D. Todd Christofferson “respected the adult victims’ wishes not to involve law enforcement at that time,” a spokesman for the Utah-based Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints told the Chicago Sun-Times recently, explaining the inaction.</p><p>But Christofferson and other church officials won’t say why the brother, Wade Christofferson, was allowed to apparently stay an active member and leader at a Latter-day Saints community in Ohio after it was known he may have abused children at some point.</p><p>Living in Dublin, Ohio, Wade Christofferson was arrested in November on child exploitation charges related to the sexual abuse of two children in Ohio and Utah over the last few years up until late 2025, court records show.</p><p>Court records indicate he’s in discussions with federal prosecutors over a potential plea agreement and wants to be released from jail pending the outcome of the criminal case so he can better attend to health problems from home.</p><p>Keith Smith, a longtime Mormon leader in Ohio, told the Sun-Times Wade Christofferson was active in the Ohio church community until last year, and while Smith knew him and his family, he had no idea of past allegations of abuse.</p><p>Smith said the congregation was informed about Wade Christofferson’s November arrest, calling it a “shock and a surprise.”</p><p>“He was released from all church responsibilities and leadership” at that time, and that “was communicated to the temple,” Smith said, describing the church’s child protection practices as “very strict.”</p><div class="Enhancement" 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/119f49f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1718x964+0+303/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc3%2F55%2F1c49cb394d45aaa6ea129f4b9fa5%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-01-at-9-03-48-pm.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/67c6738/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1718x964+0+303/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc3%2F55%2F1c49cb394d45aaa6ea129f4b9fa5%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-01-at-9-03-48-pm.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints congregation in far northwest suburban Crystal Lake, where Wade Christofferson was a onetime member and leader.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Robert Herguth / Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The alleged abuse relating to the criminal charges did not occur on church grounds and wasn’t directly related to church activities, according to records and interviews. However, court records show Wade Christofferson’s cell phone was examined around the time of his arrest and the online history included the search: “in Ohio do clergy have to report child abuse confessions”?</p><p>Court records say he also texted the father of one of his victims in November, saying: “I am deeply sorry for what I have done and will be meeting with my bishop to start the repentance process.”</p><p>While the Latter-day Saints has, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2021/2/5/22266185/blase-cupich-catholic-religious-orders-priests-abuse-archiocese-chicago-augustinians-richard-mcgrath" target="_blank" >much like the Catholic church</a>, adopted child safety protocols to prevent future abuse and deal with a sexual abuse crisis within the ranks, transparency is seen as a key part of prevention and accountability, according to experts.</p><p>If there’s an abuse allegation made against a Catholic priest in Chicago , the cleric is immediately pulled from public ministry and the parish and public are informed about the accusation while investigations are conducted. Once they’re concluded, the public is also informed of the outcome.</p><p>Most major arms of the Catholic church maintain public online registries of credibly accused clergy, but the Mormon church does not have anything similar.</p><p>While attending and serving at a Latter-day Saints congregation in far northwest suburban Crystal Lake in the mid-1990s, Wade Christofferson was “excommunicated” over allegations of child sexual abuse involving at least one minor, according to interviews.</p><p>Not long after, he was allowed to be “rebaptized,” and he moved to Ohio and resumed active participation in a Mormon community there, where he also became a leader.</p><p>Neither the Crystal Lake community nor the Ohio congregation apparently were ever told of that past misconduct.</p><div class="Figure-credit"><p>U.S. District Court </p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Referring to the turmoil in Crystal Lake and Wade Christofferson’s subsequent reinstatement, the church spokesman said: “President  D. Todd Christofferson was never in a position to know about and, in fact, did not know about or influence the ecclesiastical decisions regarding his brother’s membership.”</p><p>“While he and other family members were told of Wade’s ... excommunication, he was not informed of the specific reasons and had no reason to suspect it was for abuse.”</p><p>“It was not until around 2020 that President Christofferson first learned of — through family disclosure — some of his brother’s history of abuse from thirty years earlier.”</p><p>A Latter-day Saints web site says: “The first responsibility of the Church in abuse cases is to help those who have been abused and to protect those who may be vulnerable to future abuse.”</p><p>Utah has a mandatory reporting law that requires adults to flag police or child welfare officials over suspected child abuse, but D. Todd Christofferson apparently didn’t violate it because the victims were no longer children when he learned of the abuse, according to interviews.</p><p>Clergy can also be exempted from reporting sexual abuse if they learn about it in the context of a religious confession, but it’s unclear whether that applied here.</p><p>Child advocates say no matter the legal requirements, flagging authorities to guard against future abuse — sex offenders often don’t stop preying on children — is the right course.</p><p>Without commenting on this specific case, a child welfare expert, Laurieann Thorpe of the group Prevent Child Abuse Utah, said: "Any time we prioritize the safety of adults over children, that's a failure ... I think any time that a child is hurt and it isn’t reported and adults knew about it, it’s a failure.”</p><p>And that’s true “regardless of the time frame,” she said, referring to instances when the abuse was long ago and victims are now adults.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-800000" name="image-800000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4778078/2147483647/strip/true/crop/991x556+8+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8f%2Fec%2Fc03b37154c968d8e1a3ed92a9254%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-23-at-5-23-38-pm.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/aa2fab9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/991x556+8+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8f%2Fec%2Fc03b37154c968d8e1a3ed92a9254%2Fscreenshot-2026-03-23-at-5-23-38-pm.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Laurieann Thorpe of Prevent Child Abuse Utah, at left, and former suburban congregant Edward Nachel.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Prevent Child Abuse Utah, Mormon Stories podcast</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>A one-time member of Wade Christofferson’s McHenry County congregation, former Woodstock resident Edward Nachel, has said he was part of the church tribunal that convened in the mid-1990s over allegations Wade Christofferson molested at least one child at a “slumber party” hosted by one of his children.</p><p>Nachel said D. Todd Christofferson should have done more once he became aware of alleged abuse by Wade Christofferson.</p><p>No matter what legal reporting requirements are over abuse or church policies, Nachel said D. Todd Christofferson should have “sat him down” and told his brother: “Either you go, or I go” to the police.</p><p>D. Todd Christofferson, who is in the Mormon church’s powerful “first presidency” that also includes former Chicago attorney Dallin H. Oaks, wouldn’t speak to a reporter.</p><p>Wade Christofferson, jailed in Ohio, couldn’t be reached. His attorneys didn’t return calls and emails.</p><p>The church spokesman said that “upon learning of a recent allegation involving a minor, President Christofferson immediately reported it to legal authorities.”</p><p>It’s unclear when that was and to what agency it was reported. Court records indicate one or more parents of the child victims from 2025 initially went to police.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/03/05/mormon-church-child-sex-abuse-cover-up-crystal-lake-latter-day-saints-congregation-wade-christofferson" target="_blank" >Questions cloud Mormon church handling of suburban congregation’s ex-leader accused of abusing minors</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Robert Herguth

entries.title: Property taxes, driven by TIF districts and school funds, outpace inflation and wages: county treasurer study
entries.summary: <p>Property taxes outpaced inflation and grew “well above wage growth” over the last 30 years, shooting up nearly 182%, according to a <a class="Link" href="https://www.cookcountytreasurer.com/skyrocketingpropertytaxes.aspx" target="_blank" >new report</a> from the Cook County Treasurer’s office.</p><p>The county’s property tax levy has increased from $6.8 billion in 1995 to $19.2 billion in 2024, or twice the rate of inflation, according to Pappas. If it had remained on track with inflation, per Treasurer’s office calculations, it would be closer to $10.1 billion.</p><p>Pappas blames loopholes in the state Property Tax Extension Limitation Law, or PTELL, which was designed to limit tax increases to the rate of inflation or 5%, whichever is less.</p><p>But “local officials took advantage of that law’s loopholes to enact tax increases that substantially exceeded that limit,” and 94 of the county’s 135 municipalities aren’t bound by the law, the report says.</p><p>“The biggest culprit is what we call the levy,” Pappas said in an interview with WBEZ. “Locally, we have to cut down how much we’re spending.”</p><p>Predominantly Black neighborhoods on the South and West sides <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/real-estate/2025/11/17/cook-county-property-taxes-analysis-treasurer-maria-pappas-downtown-south-west-sides" target="_blank" ><u>were hit hardest</u></a> by the impact.</p><p>The Oakland neighborhood, just north of Kenwood, saw a 636.22% increase in property tax bills between 1995 and 2024 while East Garfield Park saw a 447.22% increase, per treasurer’s office data. The Lower West Side, West Garfield Park and North Lawndale all saw increases over 200%.</p><p>In the suburbs, the closest comparison was in unincorporated Riverside, which saw bills shoot up about 235% in that same time frame; Phoenix, Illinois saw an increase of 192.73%.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-450000" name="html-embed-module-450000"></a><div class="flourish-embed flourish-chart" data-src="visualisation/28267214"><script src="https://public.flourish.studio/resources/embed.js"></script><noscript><img src="https://public.flourish.studio/visualisation/28267214/thumbnail" width="100%" alt="visualization" /></noscript></div> </div> </div><p>In an email, Illinois Department of Revenue Director David Harris said the agency “continues to make progress on the property tax study required [by law],” which directs it and other state agencies to evaluate the state’s property tax system and recommend possible improvements to its associated processes.</p><p>There are a number of other places to put the blame, as well, according to the report, though schools and tax increment financing (TIF) districts top the list.</p><p>Schools countywide, which account for about 50% of the total tax bill, also have seen tax demands skyrocket 189%, or $6.9 billion.</p><p>Taxes imposed by Chicago Public Schools and other city governments rose from about $2.9 billion in 1995 to nearly $8.9 billion in 2024, in large part due to public pension funding mandates, the report said. Suburban municipalities added about $6.3 billion for the funds.</p><p>CPS has demanded even more money because of pension costs, which now total about $662 million a year. The city district is on the hook for 65% of its own pension costs, while suburban school districts pay 2% because the state picks up the rest for them.</p><p>And Illinois already covers a little less than a quarter of its schools’ funds, the lowest of any state.</p><p>“The schools have to make it up with property taxes,” said Hal Dardick, director of research for the treasurer’s office. “It may require some sort of alternate method by the state of funding schools.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/03/21/cook-county-assessor-fritz-kaegi-lyons-township-dan-hynes-democratic-primary-property-tax-increase-factor" >Cook County Assessor Fritz Kaegi lost big where property taxes in city, suburbs soared</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2026/02/09/illinois-lawmakers-announce-plan-aimed-at-closing-school-funding-gap" >Illinois lawmakers announce plan to close school funding gap</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>In 2017, state lawmakers set a 2027 goal to fund at least 90% of each school’s state funding needs, though it’s <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2026/02/09/illinois-lawmakers-announce-plan-aimed-at-closing-school-funding-gap" target="_blank" ><u>$5 billion short with just a year to go</u></a>. At the current rate, it will <a class="Link" href="https://www.ctbaonline.org/reports/fully-funding-evidenced-based-formula-volume-xi" target="_blank" ><u>take until at least 2037</u></a> to reach the level mandated by the original bill, closing a $3.3 billion gap, according to the Center on Tax and Budget Accountability.</p><p>Last month, state Sen. Graciela Guzmán, D-Chicago, and state Rep. Will Davis, D-Hazel Crest, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2026/02/09/illinois-lawmakers-announce-plan-aimed-at-closing-school-funding-gap" target="_blank" ><u>introduced bills</u></a> that would fulfill the funding, albeit without a plan in the legislation. Guzmán suggested a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/voter-guide-2024/2024/09/26/illinois-property-tax-relief-referendum-november-election-jb-pritzker-non-binding-pat-quinn" target="_blank" ><u>millionaire’s tax</u></a>, which former Illinois governor and treasurer Pat Quinn <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/03/21/cook-county-assessor-fritz-kaegi-lyons-township-dan-hynes-democratic-primary-property-tax-increase-factor" target="_blank" ><u>has also endorsed</u></a>.</p><p>This is while tax increases in TIF districts — which also aren’t subject to PTELL — exploded, demanding $1.3 billion more in the city and $372 million in the suburbs, an eleven-times increase, per the report. They now account for about 10% of the county’s property tax burden, up from 2.5% in 1995.</p><p>“[Legislators] need to really take a hard look at the entire tax system in the state of Illinois,” Dardick said. “In its fairness and how it harms businesses and residents.”</p>
entries.author: Violet Miller

entries.title: There is no debate over the ongoing abuse of immigrants
entries.summary: <p>Journalism is failing America.</p><p>At least mainstream media, newspapers and TV networks and such, the remnant still chugging away.</p><p>What's our job? To report the news, to convey what’s happening.</p><p>And what do we do? Describe fantasies, ghosts, processes that aren’t occurring.</p><p>Consider the recent murder of Loyola student Sheridan Gorman, out with a group of friends at a beach in Rogers Park when approached by a masked man, shot in the back and killed.</p><p>That was all too real.</p><p>But the moment the suspect was identified as a Venezuelan immigrant, the MAGA outrage machine started to whir. Led by President Donald Trump, who called him “an animal.” The murder was waved about as confirmation. Because one member of a minority group always represents everybody in that group. While white folks, naturally, are individuals, each unique in his or her own way, hardly responsible for their own actions, never mind stand-ins for anybody else.</p><p>And how did the media present this deceptive carnival? This acrobatic leap from one crime on the North Side of Chicago, one victim and one suspect, to the entire country and all immigrants everywhere?</p><p>A conversation. An argument. The Sun-Times called it “a national immigration debate.”</p><p>There is no conversation. No argument. Nobody is debating.</p><p>What is going on is dehumanization. The officially designated pariah group — nowadays, immigrants — is being stained with false rationales to justify their abuse: they are violent; they are diseased, they don't belong. If these rationals are contradictory — they are lazy and take all the good jobs — that doesn't matter. In the 1930s, the Jews were both dirty vermin crouched in the shadows and jewel-encrusted millionaires secretly running the world. Few Germans seemed bothered by the contradiction.</p><p>The nation is busily building ... let's call them “detention camps” ... for this despised group. Billions are being spent ramping up a paramilitary force to snatch people off the street. Don’t be fooled by the hesitation after Minneapolis. A temporary setback. The Democrats trying to get ICE under control is what the entire airport nightmare is about. Air travel in the United States is being throttled so we can deport more immigrants. A reminder that racism blows back on the racist. Trying to hurt others, they hurt themselves. Ask the Germans.</p><p>Why doesn't the media hit this point harder? Our error comes from a seventh grade civics class, how-a-bill-becomes-a-law mindset. It's our job to list the excuses given, noting, eventually, how inaccurate they are.</p><p>So we seek refuge recounting this Turning Point parody of debate. MAGA smugly states their premise: immigrants are violent murderers who add nothing to this country and must be forcibly expelled now. They grin condescendingly while some garble is offered in reply — in actuality, immigrants are 2 to 4 times more law-abiding, as a group, than native-born U.S. citizens. Then they twist that reply: “So now you're slandering American citizens...”</p><p>And away we go.</p><p>Words don't mean anything because they're not listening. They don’t care. As a Democrat, you’re nearly subhuman already. Maybe you’ll end up in those detention centers too. Gorman wasn’t a person, in their eyes, she’s a symbol, the latest in a list of sympathetic victims — always women — who can be performatively mourned while demanding faster deportations.</p><p>To argue is naive. It’s like the SAVE Act, the supposed election security bill. Talk all you like about the extreme rarity of voter fraud, they never reply, “Oh, right, point taken. Let’s address actual problems.” Because the bill isn’t designed to stop the fraud that isn’t there, it’s designed to constrain voting — not to help voting, but hurt it. The war against immigrants isn’t intended to stop crime; it’s intended to commit crime, against people whose lack of legal status makes them easy targets. Hurting them is called patriotism.</p><p>The ongoing horror is getting to seem so ... ordinary. Business as usual.</p><p>“Man is a creature that can get used to anything,” Dostoevsky writes in his semi-autobiographical novel, “The House of the Dead.” </p><p>That gets quoted often. Less noted is its context. The observation comes after a description of the low, stuffy room that was the narrator’s home for 10 years, sleeping on boards, amid “noise, uproar, laughter, swearing, the sound of chains, soot and fumes, shaven heads, branded faces, ragged clothes, all that is accursed and dishonored ... yes, man has great endurance.”</p><p>Is this endurance a good thing? Maybe Americans allow themselves to endure far too much. Yes, a third No Kings protest was held Saturday. Important? Sure. Maybe. Or maybe protest, like the media observing its rituals, is part of making something normal that should never be considered normal. This isn’t a debate. It’s madness. And we know where madness leads, or should.</p>
entries.author: Neil Steinberg

Supporters of the ordinance want better pay and conditions for workers.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: Chicago’s law went into effect in 2024 to increase the tipped minimum wage to parity with the city’s standard minimum wage over five years. A recent City Council vote sought to freeze tipped workers' hourly pay, before Mayor Brandon Johnson vetoed the plan last week.</p><p><b>For it</b>: "The recent backdoor effort to pause its implementation is not only harmful to tipped workers — especially Black, Brown and women workers — but also undermines the democratic process," said Raeghn Draper, organizer with the Chicago Hospitality Accountability & Advocacy Database Project and a bartender at Consignment Lounge near Logan Square.</p><p><b>Against it</b>: Gina Barge-Farmer, owner of Wax Vinyl Bar and Ramen Shop, spoke with the Sun-Times about the impact on her business. The controversy about the tipped minimum wage is "a misunderstanding of math," she said. "We’re on the hook for paying the minimum wage, irrespective." Shanell Oliver, a server at Bronzeville Winery, told the Sun-Times she earns much more with tips. "No server is working to make the hourly wage," she said.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtktdit-l-u/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p> </p><h4>LET’S HEAR FROM YOU 🗣️</h4><p><b>What do you think of freezing the minimum wage for tipped workers?</b></p><p><a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" ><i>Respondto this email</i></a><i> (please include your first and last name). rally draws thousands to Grant Park</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtktdit-l-x/" target="_blank" >Erica Thompson</a>, <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtktdit-l-m/" target="_blank" >Leigh Giangreco</a>, <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtktdit-l-c/" target="_blank" >Mary Norkol</a>, <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtktdit-l-q/" target="_blank" >Somer Van Benton</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtktdit-l-a/" target="_blank" >Mohammad Samra</a></h5><p><b>Shame of thrones?</b>: For the third time in less than a year, thousands of protesters across the Chicago area joined nationwide marches Saturday to make their voices heard regarding President Donald Trump and his policies. Downtown, protesters rallied in Grant Park before marching toward the Loop, holding signs reading, "Abolish ICE," "No Kings Since 1776" and other slogans, and ended peacefully without any major incidents at 4:30 p.m.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: An escalating conflict with Iran that has led to inflated gas prices and a lingering government shutdown that prompted the presence of U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement at various airports are among recent developments drawing widespread criticism that brought protesters to the streets as part of the latest "No Kings" demonstrations. On <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtktdit-l-z/" target="_blank" >Friday at O’Hare International Airport</a>, protesters decried ICE's presence.</p><p><b>Broadview arrests</b>: Most demonstrations were peaceful without incident. In suburban Broadview, near the ICE facility that became a flashpoint for anti-ICE protests during Operation Midway Blitz last fall, seven people who attempted to stand in the street were arrested, police said.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtktdit-l-v/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>WATCH: SATURDAY’S ‘NO KINGS’ RALLY ▶️</h4><div href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtktdit-l-jj/" target="_blank" >County property taxes have grown twice as fast as inflation over the last 30 years, study shows</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtktdit-l-jt/" target="_blank" >Nicole Jeanine Johnson</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtktdit-l-ji/" target="_blank" >Violet Miller</a></h5><p><b>Treasurer's report</b>: Property taxes outpaced inflation and grew "well above wage growth" over the last 30 years, shooting up nearly 182%, according to a new report from the Cook County Treasurer’s office. </p><p><b>The numbers</b>: The county’s property tax levy has increased from $6.8 billion in 1995 to $19.2 billion in 2024, twice the rate of inflation, according to county Treasurer Maria Pappas. If it had remained on track with inflation, per her office's calculations, that figure would be closer to $10.1 billion. Predominantly Black neighborhoods on the South and West sides <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtktdit-l-jd/" target="_blank" >were hit hardest</a>.</p><p><b>How we got here</b>: Pappas blames loopholes in the state Property Tax Extension Limitation Law, designed to limit tax increases to the rate of inflation or 5%, whichever is less. But "local officials took advantage of that law’s loopholes to enact tax increases that substantially exceeded that limit," and 94 of the county’s 135 municipalities aren’t bound by the law, the report says.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtktdit-l-jh/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>FROM THE PRESS BOX 🏀📺⚾🏈</h4><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; entries.title: Some airport bottlenecks ease as TSA says workers got their pay
entries.summary: <p>After <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/airport-travel-delays-tsa-trump-a3452b3d6a212905fab23730bbe90138" target="_blank" >weeks of chaos in U.S. airports</a>, the Transportation Safety Administration said Monday that most of its officers received much of their backpay Monday for working during the shutdown.</p><p>Weary travelers hope the overdue paychecks will lead to the end of the <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/airport-travel-delays-tsa-trump-a3452b3d6a212905fab23730bbe90138" target="_blank" >hours-long security lines</a> travelers experienced at several major U.S. airports in recent weeks.</p><p>Wait times at some TSA security bottlenecks, such as the airport checkpoints in Atlanta and Houston, already had improved significantly Monday morning.</p><p>But how long it will take for <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/shutdown-houston-airport-travel-impact-c46f4d6f49383bc9a457f3ebe001fbc1" target="_blank" >long security lines</a> to consistently return to normal — and how long federal immigration officers will maintain a visible presence in airport terminals — remains unknown as the busy spring break travel season continues.</p><p>“Working without pay forced more than 500 officers to leave TSA and thousands were forced to call out,” said Acting TSA Assistant Secretary Lauren Bis.</p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/hub/us-department-of-homeland-security" target="_blank" >DHS shutdown</a> resulted in not only <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/shutdown-houston-airport-travel-impact-c46f4d6f49383bc9a457f3ebe001fbc1" target="_blank" >travel delays</a> but also <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/airport-wait-times-shutdown-dhs-congress-tsa-391cbd731bed2e8433ab7f1a96ca9663" target="_blank" >warnings of airport closures</a> as TSA workers missing paychecks stopped going to work. Those workers were just recovering financially since last fall’s extended government shutdown.</p><p>Wait times still pushed beyond two hours at New York’s LaGuardia Airport Monday morning. Baltimore-Washington International Airport had minimal wait-times Monday morning, but continued to advise travelers to arrive three hours before their scheduled departure.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/hub/donald-trump" target="_blank" >President Donald Trump</a> on Friday ordered the Department of Homeland Security <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/shutdown-tsa-executive-order-trump-c3c57b642efa1f29307e12e038c554d0" target="_blank" >to pay TSA officers immediately</a> to ease the lines plaguing airports. The move came after Trump rejected bipartisan congressional efforts to fund the TSA while negotiations continue with <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/congress-shutdown-johnson-thune-dhs-deal-unraveled-4ad4076c09705ca4bbebbdbcac7a0e75" target="_blank" >Democrats, who have refused</a> to approve more funding without restraints on Trump’s <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/hub/immigration" target="_blank" >immigration enforcement and mass deportation operations</a>.</p><p>Democrats are demanding better identification for the officers, judicial warrants in some cases and for agents to refrain from conducting raids around schools, churches or other sensitive places. Republicans and the White House have been willing to negotiate on some points, but the sides have yet to reach a final agreement.</p><p>On Monday, there were few signs of progress on Capitol Hill, where the Senate held a short session without considering the House bill and resumed its two-week break. GOP Sen. John Hoeven of North Dakota said afterward that Senate Republicans are talking with Democrats and also the House as they try to find a way to funding DHS.</p><p>TSA employees had gone without pay since DHS funding lapsed in February. The department’s shutdown reached 44 days on Sunday, eclipsing the <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/government-shutdown-reopen-update-house-returns-5771f2befb15f4ab45e327369f2e98d9" target="_blank" >record 43-day shutdown</a> last fall that affected all of the federal government.</p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/hub/us-department-of-homeland-security" target="_blank" >DHS shutdown</a> has resulted in not only <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/shutdown-houston-airport-travel-impact-c46f4d6f49383bc9a457f3ebe001fbc1" target="_blank" >travel delays</a> but also <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/airport-wait-times-shutdown-dhs-congress-tsa-391cbd731bed2e8433ab7f1a96ca9663" target="_blank" >warnings of airport closures</a> as TSA workers missing paychecks stopped going to work. Those workers had already endured the nation’s longest government shutdown last fall. Multiple airports experienced greater than 40% callout rates, and nearly 500 of the agency’s nearly 50,000 transportation security officers quit during the shutdown.</p><p>Trump <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/airport-screening-lines-ice-trump-shutdown-dhs-9c36ce9cc31647b59867fd2b62f9379d" target="_blank" >deployed Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents</a> to some airports a week ago to help with security as TSA callouts rose nationwide. How long they stay, White House border czar Tom Homan said, depends on how quickly TSA employees return to work. A TSA statement said the agency “has immediately begun the process of paying its workforce,” with paychecks arriving “as early as Monday.”</p><p>The overall absentee rate among TSA officers scheduled to work dipped slightly on Sunday, according to DHS. The highest were concentrated at major airports that have seen consistently elevated absences lately.</p><p>Those included BWI, both of Houston’s main airports; Louis Armstrong International Airport in New Orleans; Atlanta’s Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport; and John F. Kennedy International Airport in New York.</p>
entries.author: AP

entries.title: Rams coach Sean McVay still wowed by 'stud' Caleb Williams' late-game heroics
entries.summary: <p>PHOENIX — Two months later, Rams coach Sean McVay still can’t wrap his mind around the throw <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> quarterback Caleb Williams made to force overtime in their playoff game.</p><p>“The things that are so impressive are when things go off-schedule,” he said Monday at the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/30/bears-coach-ben-johnson-uncertainty-at-left-tackle-over-next-several-years-after-ozzy-trapilo-injury" target="_blank" >NFL Annual Meeting</a>. “I mean, I couldn’t believe what I witnessed on that fourth down and four. One of the most incredible plays I’ve ever seen.”</p><p>On fourth-and-four from the 14 with 27 seconds to play in January, Williams sprinted backward before heaving a pass to tight end Cole Kmet, who camped under it for a touchdown catch. The Bears kicked an extra point to tie the game but lost in overtime.</p><p>The play was indicative of what Williams could become. Getting an endorsement from one of the most respected minds in the NFL shows how far Williams from this time last year.</p><p>“He’s a stud,” McVay said. “Just preparing for him, watching the crunch-time throws, I think he did a great job. [Coach} Ben [Johnson] does an excellent job putting a lot of pressure on defenses but he’s doing a lot of different things pre-snap. … Really impressed by that.”</p><p>Williams' late-game wizardry rubbed off on his teammates, McVay said. </p><p>“He seems like he lights up and he’s at his best in the most important moments,” said McVay, who has bumped into Williams around Los Angeles this offseason. “You can tell there’s a belief that his teammates have in him. …</p><p>“He's a fun guy to watch. I’ve got a tremendous amount of respect.. … I wish him the best — except for when they’re playing the Rams.”</p>
entries.author: Patrick Finley

entries.title: Activistas protestan contra agentes de ICE en el Aeropuerto O'Hare
entries.summary: <p>Los manifestantes se reunieron el viernes por la tarde en el Aeropuerto Internacional O’Hare para protestar contra los agentes del Servicio de Inmigración y Control de Aduanas (ICE) que han sido convocados para asistir con la seguridad del aeropuerto en medio de un cierre parcial del gobierno federal.</p><p>Los agentes de ICE <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/03/24/despliegan-a-agentes-de-ice-en-ohare-mientras-continua-el-cierre-parcial-del-gobierno-que-afecta-a-viajeros" target="_blank" >habían sido vistos</a> en O’Hare desde el lunes, luego de que el presidente Donald Trump les ordenara ayudar en las operaciones de la Administración de Seguridad en el Transporte (TSA) en aeropuertos de todo el país debido a la falta de financiamiento del Departamento de Seguridad Nacional (DHS).</p><p>No se vieron agentes de ICE el viernes en la Terminal 1, donde se reunieron un par de docenas de manifestantes, ni en la Terminal 2.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-de0000" name="module-de0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>“ICE fuera de O’Hare, ICE fuera de todas partes”, gritaron los manifestantes.</p><p>Luego de una hora de estar afuera en el área de salidas, los manifestantes ingresaron a la Terminal 1, cantando “ICE fuera ya”. Muchos viajeros y empleados de aerolíneas rieron y grabaron videos de los manifestantes. Un hombre señaló a las caras de los manifestantes y gritó, “Salgan ahora”.</p><p>Una vez que los manifestantes regresaron a su área de reunión inicial afuera, los oficiales de la Policía de Chicago emitieron una orden de dispersión por asamblea ilegal, que fue activada cuando los manifestantes entraron. Los vehículos de policía bloquearon el acceso al área de entrega de la Terminal 1. No se reportaron arrestos.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-b9defc" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Los oficiales de la Policía de Chicago explican a un pequeño grupo de manifestantes que su protesta ahora es ilegal y que deben irse o enfrentar arresto durante un mitin de emergencia que exige la salida de ICE del Aeropuerto O’Hare, el viernes 27 de marzo de 2026.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Más temprano el viernes, Trump firmó una acción ejecutiva que pagará a los trabajadores de la TSA, quienes no han recibido sueldo por semanas desde que comenzó el cierre del DHS el 14 de febrero.</p><p>TSA ha enfrentado escasez de personal durante el cierre, mientras los demócratas y republicanos en el Congreso discuten sobre el financiamiento de DHS, que supervisa la TSA, ICE y la Oficina de Aduanas y Protección Fronteriza (CBP). Los demócratas se han negado a financiar al DHS sin cambios en las prácticas de aplicación de la inmigración.</p><p>La escasez ha provocado largas filas en los puntos de control de seguridad en aeropuertos de todo el país. Sin embargo, los viajeros en O’Hare han experimentado largas filas durante los fines de semana de viajes, aunque muchos han reportado recientemente tiempos de espera de menos de media hora.</p><p>Se esperaba que aproximadamente 75 agentes de ICE estuvieran estacionados en O’Hare, dijo el alcalde Brandon Johnson a principios de esta semana. Los agentes estaban monitoreando los carriles de salida y puntos de control, pero no realizando revisiones de seguridad. Los agentes no fueron desplegados en Midway.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-ca2d38" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Melissa Espinoza, de 45 años, estuvo entre aproximadamente dos docenas de personas que protestaron en una zona de libre expresión establecida fuera de la Terminal 1 de Salidas del Aeropuerto Internacional O’Hare, exigiendo que el Servicio de Inmigración y Control de Aduanas (ICE) abandone el Aeropuerto O’Hare, el viernes 27 de marzo de 2026.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Los manifestantes del viernes señalaron la agresiva campaña de deportación de la administración de Trump el otoño pasado en el área de Chicago, el “Operativo Midway Blitz”, que trajo un aflujo de agentes de ICE y CBP que patrullaron las calles y realizaron miles de arrestos. El operativo implicó un uso aparentemente indiscriminado de gas lacrimógeno y otros irritantes químicos, así como el tiroteo fatal de Silverio Villegas González y el tiroteo de Marimar Martínez a manos de agentes federales.</p><p>“No me gusta lo que está pasando en nuestro país con los agentes de ICE aterrorizando nuestras comunidades”, dijo Melissa Espinoza, de 45 años, quien se mantuvo tranquila junto a una cerca designada para la protesta sosteniendo un letrero que decía “ICE fuera ahora”.</p><p>“Siento que es mi obligación estar aquí como persona hispana y latina”, dijo Espinoza, de Westmont. “Es importante que hable por aquellos que no tienen voz”.</p><p>Espinoza recordó haber oído historias sobre sus antepasados que huyeron de México por “sobrevivencia” y “para poder alimentar a [sus] familias”.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-620409" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Kade Heather

entries.title: Unruly passenger apparently caused Chicago-bound American Airlines flight to divert to Detroit
entries.summary: <p>Police and federal officials are investigating a "disturbance" aboard an American Airlines flight to Chicago Sunday that some passengers believe may have been a bomb threat which caused the flight to be diverted to Detroit.</p><p>The Wayne County Airport Authority Police and FBI responded to a "reported disturbance" on an American Airlines flight at Detroit Metropolitan Airport in Romulus, Michigan, authorities said. </p><p>The FBI confirmed personnel from their Detroit field office were present at the airport "conducting law enforcement activities" and noted there was no threat to the public but didn’t specify further. </p><p>American Airlines flight 2819 left John F. Kennedy Airport in New York just before 9 a.m. Eastern Standard Time and diverted to Detroit roughly two hours later, according to <a class="Link" href="https://www.flightaware.com/live/flight/AAL2819/history/20260329/1310Z/KJFK/KDTW" target="_blank" >FlightAware</a>. Several hours later, the same flight left Detroit Metropolitan Airport and touched down at O'Hare International Airport just before 7 p.m. </p><p>American Airlines has not yet responded to multiple requests for comment. </p><p>Passengers who spoke to <a class="Link" href="https://www.cbsnews.com/chicago/news/travelers-rattled-flight-ohare-diverted-unruly-passenger/" target="_blank" >CBS Chicago</a> described the tense moments that took place over 30,000 feet in the air. </p><p>"Something to the effect of, 'If you don't land this plane, I'm going to, I'll blow it the plane,' or, 'If you don't land this plane, I'm going to do something to it, and you will see,'" according to one passenger. </p><p>"And about an hour out of Chicago, we hear this person in the back screaming, yelling, and: 'No! No! No!' And he's not going to — 'I can't get off the plane!'" said another passenger. "According to the people who came on the plane, he was threatening — he said there was a bomb."</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Bears coach Ben Johnson: 'Uncertainty' at left tackle over next several years after Ozzy Trapilo injury
entries.summary: <p>PHOENIX — The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> aren’t sure who their starting left tackle will be after <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/01/11/bears-left-tackle-ozzy-trapilo-linebacker-t-j-edwards-out-for-season-after-injuries-in-packers-game" >Ozzy Trapilo</a> suffered a major knee injury in the playoffs, but the ambiguity extends well beyond this season.</p><p>Trapilo, a second-round pick a year ago, will miss most or all of the season because of a ruptured patella tendon, leaving Bears coach Ben Johnson to sort out a competition between Braxton Jones, Theo Benedet, Kiran Amegadjie and free-agent pickup Jedrick Wills.</p><p>That’s far from ideal, and Johnson left open the possibility of drafting a long-term answer at left tackle because of the unknowns with Trapilo.</p><p>“You’d love to have your long-term starter for the next 10 years locked up,” Johnson said Monday at the NFL Annual Meeting. “There’s a lot of uncertainty, though... It’s hard to say right now what that left tackle spot’s going to look like this year or five years from now.”</p><p>The Bears could target a replacement with the No. 25 pick, and if they end up with a surplus of good offensive tackles, they’ll have a trade asset.</p><p>Johnson managed a four-way battle for the spot last season and expressed frustration publicly throughout that competition. Jones opened as the starter, then got benched for Benedet, who got benched for Trapilo.</p><p>Trapilo started the final six regular-season games and the wild-card game against the Packers and made enough progress to take hold of the position for the foreseeable future before the injury.</p><p>Jones, who started 40 games over his first three seasons, appears to be the leader at left tackle for now. He missed the end of the 2024 season with a broken ankle that disrupted his ensuing offseason training and wasn’t back to full strength at the start of last season. He also got hurt in practice during the season and ended up playing just six games.</p><p>However, with the Bears short on salary-cap space and limited in draft capital, they re-signed him to a one-year deal.</p><p>“He still didn’t feel 100% like himself,” Johnson said. “What he put on tape those first four games, he didn’t feel like was very reflective of what he’s capable of. Looking back at it, we would agree with that. There’s more in there than what we saw.</p><p>“He came in when he signed the contract [March 13] and he looked like a brand-new man. This guy was beefed up... He’s very determined to get his career trajectory back to where it was before.”</p>
entries.author: Jason Lieser

entries.title: A look at the best players, teams and stories from the high school basketball season
entries.summary: <p>The high school basketball season is a wrap. Here is the biggest and best from the 2025-26 season.</p><p><b>Best team: Marist</b></p><p>After finishing third in the East Suburban Catholic Conference, the state champions of the state’s largest class put it all together at the right time.</p><p>How dominating were the RedHawks over the final three weeks of the season? The average victory margin in seven postseason wins was 20 points a game with just one game closer than 12 points.</p><p><b>Best player: Jaxson Davis, Warren</b></p><p>The poised and polished point guard continues to prove over and over again he’s the real deal. Davis, who averaged 25 points, six rebounds and five assists a game, blends being a high-feel orchestrator and playmaker with scoring ability as well as anyone.</p><p><b>Biggest surprise: York</b></p><p>At each step of the way this season, coach Mike Dunn’s team was always referenced to as “surprising York.” That mantra kept up throughout the postseason and all the way to Champaign. No one would have forecast a top four finish in 4A for the Dukes when the season began.</p><p><b>Mr. March: Amarion Smith-Holley, Peoria Richwoods</b></p><p>There was no player who put his team on his back and carried it more than the sophomore point guard.</p><p>Smith-Holley put up 31 in a regional final win over Rock Island. Down two in a sectional semifinal against Normal, he hit three pressure-packed free-throws with no time on the clock and scored 29 points for a 68-67 win. In the sectional final, Smith-Holley’s key late steal and two free throws with 12 second left lifted the Knights to a 59-58 win over Edwardsville as he finished with 21 points.</p><p><b>Biggest story: Continued rise of private schools</b></p><p>Remember, there was very little overall impact from private schools in basketball for decades in this state. Sure, there was a team here and there that made its presence felt over the years, but the reign of state basketball power, specifically the depth, has shifted dramatically.</p><p>The Chicago Catholic League was clearly the best conference in the state. There were three private schools among the final four teams in 4A. If not for York’s overtime win over St. Ignatius in the supersectional, it would have been all private schools in 4A.</p><p>And the state's largest class featured its first-ever all-private school state championship game when Marist beat Benet.</p><p><b>Breakout senior: Charles Barnes, Marist</b></p><p>A promising player since his arrival as a freshman at De La Salle, the 6-5 Barnes put it all together as a senior after transferring to Marist. His final season included significant numbers, all-state recognition and a state championship.</p><p><b>Breakout junior: Mason Martin, Neuqua Valley</b></p><p>The season started out with an attention-grabbing bang for the 6-5 sharpshooter. A 41-point performance with 11 three-pointers in the season opener will do that for you.</p><p>What followed was an offer and commitment to Illinois and a monster junior campaign. Martin, who averaged 19 points a game, dazzled as a shooter, connecting on a whopping 118 three-pointers at a 45 percent clip. He also made 85 of 91 free throws (93 percent).</p><p><b>Breakout sophomore: Amir Tucker, St. Ignatius</b></p><p>Tucker put himself on the map and was more impactful than anticipated. His improvement, production and clutch play down the stretch stood out.</p><p>The 6-2 guard wanted the ball at crunch time and filled the stat sheet, ending the year averaging 12.1 points a game. He also drained 62 three-pointers while shooting 38 percent from beyond the arc.</p>
entries.author: Joe Henricksen

entries.title: League source says Bulls working on exit strategy from Jaden Ivey
entries.summary: <p>SAN ANTONIO – The Bulls wanted to see more from Jaden Ivey than just the four games he gave them before his knee issues became a concern and he was shut down.</p><p>As of Monday, however, there is nothing the organization wants to see from him any longer.</p><p>According to an NBA source, because of Ivey’s latest social media comments the Bulls were working with the league on an exit strategy from the former No. 5 overall pick (2022 draft).</p><p>Acquired at the trade deadline from Detroit in the Kevin Huerter deal, there were some red flags around Ivey last month when he held an impromptu postgame sermon about the “Old Jaden Ivey” being dead, especially now that “Christ was in his life.”</p><p>Over the last few weeks, he started taking to his social media account and preaching to people his beliefs, while condemning those that he felt weren’t “saved.”</p><p>But on Monday came the final straw when he went on a rant about the LGBTQ, saying, “The world proclaims LGBTQ, right? They proclaim Pride Month and the NBA does too. They show it to the world. They say, ‘Come join us for Pride Month to celebrate unrighteousness.’ “</p><p></p>
entries.author: Joe Cowley

entries.title: Chicago sees record-breaking heat ahead of cold front Tuesday
entries.summary: <p>Chicagoans were greeted by unseasonably warm temperatures Monday, but a cold front is expected the hit the area on Tuesday.</p><p>The National Weather Service recorded a temperature of 80 degrees at O'Hare Airport, surpassing the previous record of 79 degrees set in 1986 and met in 1998, according to NWS meteorologist Mark Ratzer.</p><p>But he warned not to get used to the heat.</p><p>A cold front heading to the area Tuesday afternoon is expected to cause temperatures to plummet to a low of 37 degrees by Tuesday night. Areas near the lake will likely be cooler than inland areas.</p><p>Showers and thunderstorms are also possible in the area early Tuesday and throughout much of the rest of the week. Temperatures rivaling Monday's record likely won't return to the area within the next week, according to the Weather Service.</p>
entries.author: Mary Norkol

entries.title: Protesta 'Sin Reyes' atrae a miles al Grant Park de Chicago
entries.summary: <p>Por tercera vez en menos de un año, miles de manifestantes en el área de Chicago se unieron a marchas nacionales el sábado para hacer escuchar su voz respecto al presidente Donald Trump y sus políticas.</p><p>Un conflicto en escalada con Irán que ha llevado a un aumento en los precios de la gasolina y un cierre gubernamental que provocó la presencia del Servicio de Inmigración y Control de Aduanas (ICE) en varios aeropuertos a principios de esta semana son algunos de los desarrollos más recientes que han suscitado críticas generalizadas y que llevaron a los manifestantes a las calles como parte de las últimas manifestaciones “Sin Reyes (“No Kings”).</p><p>La protesta en Grant Park de Chicago comenzó el sábado por la tarde con una poderosa interpretación de “A Change Is Gonna Come” y una canción original titulada “In This America” de la artista local Melody Angel.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-230000" name="module-230000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Escribió la canción en memoria de Trayvon Martin, cuyo asesinato en 2012 galvanizó el movimiento Black Lives Matter. Ahora dedica la canción a su sobrina y sobrino.</p><p>"Este no es el mundo que quiero para ellos, y lo cambiaré,” dijo Angel desde el escenario.</p><p>Para la 1:30 p.m., miles se reunieron en el Butler Field en Grant Park. Los asistentes sostenían carteles de “Abolir ICE” y “No Kings Since 1776” (“Sin reyes desde 1776”) mientras canciones como “Fight the Power” y “I Will Survive” sonaban a través de las bocinas.</p><div data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Miles marchan por el Loop el sábado como parte del movimiento de protesta nacional “Sin Reyes” (“No Kings”) en contra de la administración de Trump.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Para las 3 p.m., la multitud comenzó a marchar por el Loop sosteniendo grandes letreros que incluían “ICE OUT”, (“Fuera ICE”) antes de finalizar pacíficamente sin ningún incidente importante a las 4:30 p.m.</p><p>La manifestación es parte de un movimiento nacional, denominado “Sin Reyes” (“<a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/no-kings-protests-minnesota-trump-springsteen-2fab6b3a64e5275bcf111e8dd6d2e075" target="_blank" >No Kings</a>”), para protestar las políticas de la administración de Trump, incluyendo las deportaciones masivas, el retroceso de los derechos de las personas transgénero y la guerra en Irán. Las primeras protestas de “Sin Reyes”, que tuvieron lugar en junio pasado, estaban planeadas para contrarrestar un desfile militar en Washington, D.C. para conmemorar el 250 aniversario del Ejército y el cumpleaños 79 de Trump.</p><p>La manifestación del Loop fue organizada por la Coalición Hands Off Chicago, que incluye la Federación de Trabajo de Chicago, Equality Illinois, la Coalición por los Derechos de Inmigrantes y Refugiados de Illinois (ICIRR), Indivisible Chicago Alliance y otros, así como la Unión Estadounidense por las Libertades Civiles (ACLU) de Illinois. Los voluntarios dijeron que se esperaba una multitud de 250,000 a 300,000 personas, aunque la multitud parecía más pequeña que la del otoño pasado.</p><p>En octubre, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2025/06/16/protestas-no-reyes-en-chicago-y-en-todo-el-pais-llenan-calles-para-oponerse-a-las-politicas-de-trump" target="_blank" >decenas de miles de personas asistieron a protestas similares</a> en toda la región de Chicago. Los republicanos habían descrito las protestas como el “Hate America Rally.”</p><p>Reflexionando sobre la progresión de las protestas “Sin Reyes” durante el último año, Laura Tanner, de Indivisible Chicago, dijo que el movimiento de resistencia sigue creciendo.</p><p>“Cada vez más personas están horrorizadas y activas, y sólo quieren ser parte de estas manifestaciones masivas”, destacó.</p><div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>El alcalde Brandon Johnson habla el sábado durante un mitin en Grant Park antes de que los manifestantes marchen por el Loop como parte del movimiento de protesta nacional “Sin Reyes” (“No Kings”) contra la administración de Trump.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>El alcalde Brandon Johnson, quien subió brevemente al escenario en Grant Park, hizo un llamado para poner fin al interminable “asalto” contra los inmigrantes y los trabajadores. También anunció un día de acción que se llevará a cabo el 1 de mayo para presionar a los “ultra ricos” para que “paguen su parte justa en impuestos”.</p><p>“Mira a tu alrededor”, dijo. “Nuestro movimiento es más grande. Nuestra determinación es más fuerte. Y estamos enviando un mensaje: No hay reyes en Estados Unidos”.</p><p>Natanael Hosen, de South Barrington, llegó a los Estados Unidos desde Argentina cuando tenía 19 años y “logró el sueño americano.”</p><p>Hosen, de 37 años, comenzó su empresa y está criando a cinco hijos, pero él y su familia han enfrentado amenazas de deportación desde el inicio de los operativos de aplicación de la ley de inmigración de Trump.</p><p>“Me siento como si estuviera haciendo algo malo solo por existir”, dijo Hosen. “No puedo ir a los aeropuertos. No puedo viajar”.</p><p>Hosen, quien se siente “en riesgo” por primera vez en sus 18 años en Estados Unidos, dijo que el bombardeo de Irán fue el “punto de quiebre” para asistir a una manifestación.</p><p>“Tengo amigos en otros países, y se están riendo de nosotros”, destacó Hosen. “Simplemente aceptamos lo que nos dan, y esto en algún momento tiene que detenerse”.</p><div
