srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6062a0f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5472x3071+0+288/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F81%2F74%2Fee09263248f28b939f4420201bb3%2Fdji-0004.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a4cd64e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5472x3071+0+288/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F81%2F74%2Fee09263248f28b939f4420201bb3%2Fdji-0004.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6062a0f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5472x3071+0+288/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F81%2F74%2Fee09263248f28b939f4420201bb3%2Fdji-0004.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Para la 1:30 p.m. el sábado, miles de personas comenzaron a reunirse en Grant Park en Chicago para dar inicio a la marcha y el mitin “Sin Reyes”.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Linda Tucker, de 70 años, residente de Kenwood, dijo que asistió para expresar muchas quejas, incluyendo que un número desproporcionado de <a class="Link" href="https://iwpr.org/iwpr-new-report-black-women-disproportionately-sidelined-in-year-one-of-trumps-second-term/" target="_blank" >mujeres afroamericanas han sido excluidas del mercado laboral</a> desde que Trump inició su segundo mandato.</p><p>Dijo que se sentía desalentada por la composición de la multitud.</p><p>“Hay tantas personas de mi generación aquí y no suficientes jóvenes”, agregó. “Mi generación vivió asesinatos, la Guerra de Vietnam, [Richard] Nixon y [Ronald] Reagan, y sabemos lo difícil que fue conseguir lo que tuvimos. No hay razón para darlo por hecho”.</p><p>El sábado en toda el área de Chicago, muchos participantes llevaron a sus hijos a las manifestaciones, enfatizando la importancia de enseñarles a alzar su voz. Aunque muchos expresaron frustración con Trump, algunos dijeron que encontraron consuelo en la solidaridad que los rodeaba.</p><p>Los organizadores dijeron que más de 3,100 eventos —500 más que en octubre— se registraron en los 50 estados, con protestas también realizándose en Europa, según informó la AP. Se esperaba que la protesta en Grant Park fuera la más grande en Chicago, pero se llevaron a cabo manifestaciones más pequeñas en Lincoln Square e Irving Park. Fuera de la ciudad, otras manifestaciones tuvieron lugar en Cicero, Oak Park y Highland Park.</p><p>La mayoría de las demostraciones fueron pacíficas y sin incidentes. En Broadview, un suburbio cerca de la instalación de ICE, que se convirtió en un punto focal para las protestas anti ICE durante el Operativo Midway Blitz el otoño pasado, siete personas que intentaron pararse en la calle en 25th Avenue y Harvard fueron arrestadas, según la Policía.</p><p></p><h3><b>‘Ya hemos estado aquí antes’ </b></h3><p>Mientras Joanne Bertalan se sentaba en su silla de ruedas durante la manifestación en Evanston el sábado por la mañana, recordó las protestas por los derechos civiles a las que asistió como estudiante universitaria en la Universidad Bradley en 1964.</p><p>“Ya hemos estado aquí antes”, dijo Bertalan, de 77 años. “Estas personas han vivido muchas cosas y guerras. Hay muchos veteranos aquí y están cansados de esto”.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-8a3098" name="image-8a3098"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c81f6de/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fee%2F11%2F2f7feef54feebbf72293e5c355a0%2Fimg-7890.jpeg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b7af7d6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fee%2F11%2F2f7feef54feebbf72293e5c355a0%2Fimg-7890.jpeg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/71948ae/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fee%2F11%2F2f7feef54feebbf72293e5c355a0%2Fimg-7890.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Cheree Bertalan (izquierda) se sienta junto a su madre, Joanne Bertalan, durante la manifestación “Sin Reyes” del sábado en el Dawes Park en Evanston. La madre y la hija caminaron con su iglesia para unirse a la protesta." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/71948ae/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fee%2F11%2F2f7feef54feebbf72293e5c355a0%2Fimg-7890.jpeg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/025e9f5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fee%2F11%2F2f7feef54feebbf72293e5c355a0%2Fimg-7890.jpeg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/71948ae/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fee%2F11%2F2f7feef54feebbf72293e5c355a0%2Fimg-7890.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Cheree Bertalan (izquierda) se sienta junto a su madre, Joanne Bertalan, durante la manifestación “Sin Reyes” del sábado en el Dawes Park en Evanston. La madre y la hija caminaron con su iglesia para unirse a la protesta.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Leigh Giangreco/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>La manifestación suburbana también incluyó al alcalde de Evanston, Daniel Biss, el reciente ganador de las primarias demócratas para el escaño del Distrito 9 Congresional de Illinois, quien subió al escenario a eso de las 10:45 a.m.</p><p>“Las cosas han empeorado”, dijo Biss. “Hay una guerra, una guerra injusta, ilegal y desquiciada. Hemos soportado una violenta ocupación federal en nuestras calles, aquí en Evanston y en todo el área de Chicago durante el otoño, y luego fueron a Minneapolis, donde simplemente comenzaron a disparar a la gente”, dijo, refiriéndose a Alex Pretti y Renee Good, quienes fueron asesinados a tiros por agentes federales en Minnesota.</p><p></p><h3><b>Luchando por derechos en Cicero </b></h3><p>Afuera del Ayuntamiento de Cicero, una multitud de 60 personas se reunió el sábado.</p><p>“¡No odio, no miedo, los inmigrantes son bienvenidos aquí!” gritaron en un momento, mientras los autos en Cermak Road tocaban la bocina en aprobación.</p><p>Un veterano de los Marines de Estados Unidos, que se identificó como José Manuel, habló en la manifestación sobre la cantidad de dinero que Trump ha gastado en la guerra en Irán y el conflicto entre Israel y Hamas en Gaza.</p><p>“Cuando solicitamos inversiones aquí en casa para escuelas, vivienda y atención de salud mental, nos dicen que no hay suficiente”, dijo el hombre de 38 años. “Pero siempre hay suficiente dinero para la guerra”.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-2d739f" name="image-2d739f"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" Cicero.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>Niños se unen a la manifestación de Lincoln Square </b></h3><p>Cerca de 100 personas, incluidos niños, se reunieron más temprano el sábado en Welles Park en Lincoln Square. La gente llevó a sus hijos y perros, y utilizaron gises para escribir mensajes como “Elige amor” y “Ama a tu prójimo”. El grupo cantó, “Esto es por nuestra gente que está encerrada, juntos aboliremos ICE”, antes de marchar por el perímetro del parque.</p><p>Brenna O’Brien, una madre local que organizó la protesta como una alternativa a la manifestación en Grant Park, dirigió al grupo antes de la marcha.</p><p>“No le somos leales a un rey loco que intenta apoderarse del país”, dijo entre vítores. “Los habitantes de Chicago se cuidan mutuamente y no dejaremos que él se apodere de esta ciudad”.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a entries.author: Mohammad Samra

entries.title: NFL getting into the pro flag football business
entries.summary: <p>PHOENIX — The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/halas-intrigue/2026/03/30/bears-nfl-annual-meetings-podcast-halas-intrigue" target="_blank" >NFL</a> is getting into the pro flag football business.</p><p>The league announced Monday that it, along with partner TMRW Sports, will develop and launch leagues for both men and women. The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >NFL</a> toured a list of famous alumni as investors, including former quarterbacks Peyton Manning, Joe Montana, Steve Young and Eli Manning, and current players Arik Armstead, Russell Wilson and Bobby Wagner. </p><p>Former receiver Larry Fitzgerald, who played for the Cardinals and still lives in the Valley of the Sun, was part of Monday’s announcement at the Arizona Biltmore. Fitzgerald praised the move, calling himself a former flag player.</p><p>Former tennis stars Billie Jean King and Serena Williams and former soccer star Alex Morgan were also listed among the investors.</p><p>The NFL has for years been interested in flag football and sees the potential for growth leading up to the 2028 Summer Olympics, which will feature the sport. The NFL estimates that 4.1 million kids in the United States play flag football.</p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" entries.author: Jason Lieser

entries.title: Are Chicago’s theater awards broken? The Jeffs face growing backlash
entries.summary: <p>A week after “boos” rained down in <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/theater/2026/03/23/chicago-theater-awards-non-equity-jeff-winners-2026-kokandy-invictus" target="_blank" ><u>a ceremony</u></a> honoring some of Chicago’s top storefront theater companies, the Joseph Jefferson Awards — known conversationally as “the Jeffs” and akin to the local Tonys — are facing backlash.</p><p>Several theaters said on social media and in interviews over the weekend that they are stepping away from the Jeffs, established in 1968 to reward excellence in Chicago’s vibrant theater scene. And at least one Jeffs committee member has publicly resigned.</p><p>Today, the Jeff Awards are split into two annual award ceremonies: the Non Equity Jeffs honor Chicago storefront theater companies, and the Equity Jeffs honor larger regional theaters, with performers in the actors’ union. The Jeff Awards are staffed by an all-volunteer organization consisting of judges attending performances and making recommendations for Jeff-recommended shows and award considerations.</p><p>In a statement signed by members of the Jeff committee Sunday, the group pledged to review its judging and voting practices and incorporate what it learns from outreach to the larger community. A new subcommittee tasked with the review will report findings by July 1.</p><p>“The Jeff Awards exist for one reason: to spotlight the artists and creatives who make Chicago the most vibrant theater city in the world,” the statement read. “When the community we serve is hurting, we feel a profound responsibility to listen, to reflect, and to evolve<i>.”</i></p><p>The controversy erupted last week after the 52nd annual Non Equity Jeff Award ceremony. The committee of judges honored Invictus Theatre’s artistic director Charles Askenaizer as best director. Askenaizer is under investigation by the theater company’s board after recent <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/p/DUJy_-eiart/?hl=en" target="_blank" ><u>allegations of abuse surfaced on social media from actors</u></a>. </p><p>Askenaizer was not immediately available for comment, a spokesperson said. But in a statement, Invictus Theatre’s board said it has hired a third party to examine allegations and will “cooperate fully with the investigation.” The statement continued: “We recognize that some in our community feel frustrated that we have not responded publicly on social media to these allegations. This type of situation is new to us, and we are doing our best to navigate it thoughtfully and with respect to all parties involved. Our goal is not speed, but ensuring the matter is addressed appropriately and with care.”</p><p>Theoretically, winning a Jeff Award can boost a theater's profile. It can lead to further employment opportunities for individual artists, and these awards are often used in promotional materials for companies.</p><p>But the issues many storefront companies and artists have with the Jeffs didn’t begin, or end, with the 2026 honors, some theater artists who protested the group said in interviews.</p><p>The looming questions, then, underneath all of the recent blowback: Are the Jeff Awards broken? And, after over a half-century in operation, what purpose do these awards serve today?</p><p>Siblings Xavier M. Custodio and Yajaira Custodio are co-founders of Vision Latino Theatre Company. <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/visionlatino/p/DWUudFpmvDn/" target="_blank" ><u>They announced last week on Instagram</u></a> they are stepping away from the Jeff Awards.</p><p>Xavier said his company’s decision to cut ties with the local awards institution started in October of 2025.</p><div border-radius: 4px; flex-grow: 0; height: 14px; width: 144px;"></div></div></a></div></blockquote> <script async src="//platform.instagram.com/en_US/embeds.js"></script> </div> </div></div><p>“We had just finished doing a play [“Las Borinqueñas”] about the birth control trials that happened in Puerto Rico, and my sister was sitting next to one of the Jeffs committee members who was sleeping right when the show started,” he said.</p><p>Yajaira said the sleeping committee member wasn’t the first incident. Committee members were rude in past shows, “asking about run time, and saying, ‘Oh, that's a little too long,’ and just being rude in general,” she said.</p><p>They also questioned the cultural competency of the judges. Xavier asked the committee via email how they evaluate shows. The response, he said, was that, “it’s all technical.”</p><p>“It's a very Eurocentric way of looking at theater,” he said. “So a lot of theaters of color go through a lot of this where we don't get recommended, or we don't get nominated or win, if it's not fitting their Eurocentric vision of what theater should be.”</p><p>When the Custodios reached out to the committee to complain, the committee reportedly refused to send another judge, instead opting to “do better in the future,” Xavier said.</p><p>WBEZ/Chicago Sun-Times asked the Jeff Awards committee for information regarding age, race and gender demographics of Jeff judges and voters for this story. The committee did not send a response by publication time.</p><p>Some storefront theater administrators said they are questioning what they get from Jeff recommendations. Tony Lawry, founding artistic director of Theatre Above Law, another company that has recently decided to <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/theatreabovethelaw/p/DWUmXVWlDl8/" target="_blank" ><u>cut ties with the Jeffs</u></a>, said his company did an evaluation of its four Jeff-recommended shows.</p><p>“For those four productions that were Jeff-recommended, literally, less than five tickets were sold due to the recommendation,” he said. “We always ask when people buy tickets online, ‘How did you hear about the show?’ So I went back and looked at those question tag results, and basically one ticket per production responded that it was sold because we were Jeff-recommended. That's not worth it.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <blockquote class="instagram-media" #F4F4F4; border-left: 8px solid transparent; transform: translateY(-4px) translateX(8px);"></div></div></div> <div style="display: flex; flex-direction: column; flex-grow: 1; justify-content: center; margin-bottom: 24px;"> <div style=" background-color: #F4F4F4; border-radius: 4px; flex-grow: 0; height: 14px; margin-bottom: 6px; width: 224px;"></div> <div style=" background-color: #F4F4F4; border-radius: 4px; flex-grow: 0; height: 14px; width: 144px;"></div></div></a></div></blockquote> <script async src="//platform.instagram.com/en_US/embeds.js"></script> </div> </div></div><p>Lawry said Jeff Award judges also fell asleep in productions staged by his theater company.</p><p>“We've had Jeff judges that probably shouldn't be allowed to be Jeff judges, because they can't stay awake through a whole show,” he said. “And we don't do shows that are longer than 90 minutes.”</p><p>After the flurry of social media comments last week, the Jeffs committee said in its statement that the group acknowledged its most recent misstep in awarding an artist under investigation. </p><p>“We realize that our initial silence felt out of step with the urgency of these concerns and for this we apologize; our intent is always to support this community, never to diminish the importance of safety or well-being," the committee said.</p><p>This silence is the primary reason David Price, a former opening night judge for the committee, decided to step down.</p><p>“However well-intentioned a decision may be to step back and remain silent, and however one may wish that that silence is interpreted as neutrality, the reality is that silence is never interpreted by most people as neutrality,” Price said. “There's no neutrality on this issue. You're either working to make things better, or, by your lack of being part of the process, you're allowing things to remain the same or get worse.”</p><p>For some theaters, the Jeff committee’s response may be too little too late.</p><p>“They claim they care about the community, but they obviously don't,” Lawry said.</p>
entries.author: Mike Davis | WBEZ

entries.title: Suspect who allegedly sexually assaulted woman near Loop parking garage arrested
entries.summary: <p>Charges were pending for a man police took into custody shortly after he allegedly sexually assaulted a woman near a Loop parking garage Monday morning, according to Chicago police.</p><p>The woman, 20, was walking in the first block of North Michigan Avenue at 6:35 a.m. when the man approached her and assaulted her, police said. Witnesses intervened and the man fled before being arrested by police shortly after. </p><p>The woman was taken to Northwestern Memorial Hospital, police said.</p><p>A spokesperson for Millennium Garages, which is in the first block of North Michigan Avenue, told a reporter they would have information to release at a later time. </p><p>As the situation folded, “multiple” 911 calls reporting a criminal sexual assault were received by the city’s Office of Emergency Management and Communications, police scanner transmissions showed.</p><p>A woman who says she parks in the garage often — and declined to give her name for safety and privacy reasons — said she brings her dog when she parks in the garage late at night.</p><p>Another woman, who similarly declined to give her name and works nearby, said people often take shelter in the garage lobby but no one had ever been threatening or violent in her experience.</p><p>Charges are pending.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: CPS Board approves contract for new CEO Macquline King with just 1 ‘no’ vote
entries.summary: <p>The Chicago Board of Education approved a contract for the school district’s new CEO Macquline King, a homegrown talent who has led the district on an interim basis for the last 10 months.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://www.cps.edu/press-releases/2026/march/chicago-board-of-education-selects-dr.-macquline-king-as-superintendentceo-of-chicago-public-schools/" target="_blank" ><u>King will have a three-year contract</u></a> with a starting salary of $380,000, which is $40,000 more than her predecessor Pedro Martinez.</p><p>The vote represented a rare coming together of the partially elected, partially appointed school board. Only Jennifer Custer, who represents the Far Northwest Side, voted against King’s contract.</p><p>After the near-unanimous vote, King got a standing ovation.</p><p>King said she has confidence that Chicago Public Schools is moving in the right direction despite “the serious challenges” the district faces. She said she will work to build on the progress students have made in graduating on time from high school and earning college credit from dual enrollment classes.</p><p>Now, she said, the mission is to “build a bridge of excellence” across the K-12 experience, ensuring there’s a strong curriculum for every subject.</p><p>“We will protect the progress that we’ve made with everything we have,” King said. “Our path forward is clear, it's etched on our strategic plan centered on one thing: student experience.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Chicago school board members took a selfie with new CEO Macquline King after voting to approve her three-year contract, bringing to an end a monthslong search for a permanent leader.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“Most importantly, I will listen to our students,” King added. “I want every child to feel a deep sense of belonging at our schools. As a proud CPS graduate, I see myself reflected in the eyes of our students.”</p><p>King was <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2025/11/05/cps-ceo-search-macquline-king" target="_blank" ><u>not originally named as a finalist</u></a> for the permanent position, but board members <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2026/03/13/cps-board-names-3-finalists-to-lead-district-interim-ceo-king-former-nyc-east-baton-rouge-schools-chiefs" target="_blank" ><u>added her to the shortlist a few weeks ago</u></a>. Support quickly coalesced around King, especially after a former chancellor of New York City’s public schools <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2026/03/24/finalist-for-cps-ceo-sito-narcisse-courted-controversy-in-past-jobs-but-defenders-praise-his-vision" target="_blank" ><u>dropped out of contention</u></a>.</p><p>Custer said she was disappointed that King was added back into the mix at the last minute.</p><p>“All of a sudden we have a lot of pressure right at the very tail end to kind of get her back in,” Custer said. “This really wasn't the process that the people of Chicago were looking for, and this wasn't the process that we had laid out.”</p><p>She also said school staff in her district told her that they thought King’s communication was lacking, describing it as “not as frequent, not as often, kind of last-minute.”</p><p>So far, Custer is the only sitting member to announce a run for school board president. She said if she wins, she would not automatically seek to replace King, adding that she wants to give her a chance to prove herself.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>School board member Jennifer Custer was the lone ‘no’ vote to appoint Macquline King as CPS CEO. She said school staff in her Far Northwest Side district had complained that King’s communication was lacking.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>Board concludes ‘long strange trip’ to hire permanent CEO</h3><p>Michilla Blaise, who was appointed by Mayor Brandon Johnson, said King checks all her boxes: A Black woman who lives on the West Side and <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2026/03/28/macquline-king-cps-grad-and-principal-who-led-through-mass-school-closures-rises-to-ceo" target="_blank" ><u>has deep experience in CPS</u></a>.</p><p>She also said she believes that King will work with her to figure out how to address under-enrolled schools, one of the major issues facing the school district and the West Side communities that Blaise represents. Blaise said it would take “true partnership” to come up with a vision for those schools and see it “through to fruition without just closing doors.”</p><p>Ellen Rosenfeld, who represents the Near North Side, said she has supported King from the beginning, but noted the <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/chicago-school-board/2026/02/13/is-cps-superintendent-search-on-track-or-stalled-depends-who-you-ask-on-school-board" target="_blank" ><u>monthslong search for a permanent leader</u></a> had been “a long strange trip.”</p><p>Rosenfeld said she was impressed with King’s bravery and independence, likely a reference to when King stood up to the mayor and his allies on the school board by refusing to present a budget that could have put the cash-strapped district further into debt.</p><p>“We will continue to demand transparency, accountability and meaningful engagement,” Rosenfeld said. “Congratulations, let's get to work.”</p><p>Yesenia Lopez, whose Southwest Side district was frequently targeted at the height of immigration enforcement activity last fall, said she saw King’s leadership grow during that time. </p><p>“She was open to collaborate on the best response possible at the time when Midway Blitz was operating, hitting many of our… immigrant communities,” Lopez said.</p><p>But she asked King to include Latino and Asian American leaders in her cabinet so her advisers would represent the diversity of the district’s students and “understand the importance of our English learners, of our diverse communities, of special education, [and the] Safe Passage program.”</p><h3>Many praise school board’s decision to hire ‘one of their own’</h3><p>King also got endorsements from union leaders, community members, and several City Council members.</p><p>Dian Palmer, president of SEIU Local 73, which represents thousands of CPS workers, said her members had faith that King will be the kind of leader who can “sit across the table from us and treat our support staff not as a line item in the budget, but as the beating heart of this system.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-870000" name="image-870000"></a> class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Dian Palmer, president of SEIU Local 73, said she hoped new CEO Macquline King would look to her “lived experience” as a CPS principal, parent and student as she leads the district.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“When we talk about who should lead those schools, we don't just talk about a title,” she said. “We talk about a vision, we talk about values.”</p><p>Like several other speakers, Palmer said she believes King’s “lived experience” in CPS will serve her well.</p><p>King is a CPS graduate and her two grown sons attended CPS. She taught in Chicago before becoming principal of two small elementary schools — one mostly Black school on the South Side and one a diverse school on the North Side. She has experience leading through <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/chicagos-50-closed-schools" target="_blank" ><u>CPS’ 2013 mass school closures</u></a>, welcoming children to a new school after theirs closed and leading through the COVID-19 pandemic.</p><p>“She knows that sustainable progress in our schools cannot be won through top-down mandates,” Palmer said. “It is built brick by brick through honest action, mutual respect and shared sacrifice.”</p><p>Kia Banks, president of the Chicago Principals and Administrators Association, said King has demonstrated a willingness to work with school leaders. The group became an official collective bargaining union for CPS principals in 2023 and finalized its first contract in the fall under King.</p><p>“We recognize that we will not always agree,” Banks said. “There will be moments of tension. There will be difficult decisions, and there will always be competing priorities.”</p><p>But Banks said she is confident that the unions, the school board and King can find common ground to move the school district forward.</p><p>Jackson Potter, the vice president of the Chicago Teachers Union, said he is hoping that King’s elevation to CEO represents a change from when past CPS leaders “kowtowed to mega-millionaires and privateers.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-db0000" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Jackson Potter, vice president of the Chicago Teachers Union, said Macquline King had shown herself to be a different kind of leader and applauded how she’s so far handled the midyear closure of two charter high schools. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Potter said King showed she was different from former CEOs in how she dealt with Aspira, a charter school network set to close this week after running out of money.</p><p>“Your work to ensure that Aspira students graduate with as little disruption as possible, despite the grave and irresponsible actions of the charter operator, is to be lauded,” he said.</p><p>Union leaders and City Council members also brought up King’s stewardship of the budget last year. She inherited a $734 million deficit when she took over as interim CEO in June, but refused to present a budget that included the possibility of borrowing, as the mayor’s office wanted.</p><p>Ald. Stephanie Coleman of the 16th Ward congratulated King on behalf of the Chicago Aldermanic Black Caucus.</p><p>“We couldn’t be more proud of the accomplishments that she has made, especially in the last most trying budget,” Coleman said. “Dr. King, you have done a herculean job with balancing the budget, no furlough days, no school days out, and I commend you and your team.”</p><p>20th Ward Ald. Jeanette Taylor, who chairs the city council’s education committee , said she was “excited” that the board picked “one of their own” to lead the district.</p><p>“We know that the board has made the right decision, and I am excited that we are going to get back to information and inspiration,” Taylor said.</p><p>State Rep. Ann Williams, whose district includes swaths of the North Side, said she was “thrilled” about King’s appointment, and applauded her for making herself available to state lawmakers at a level that “we have not seen in a number of years” from the head of CPS.</p><p>“That partnership, that collaboration I think is going to be invaluable as we struggle to meet the funding challenges and some of the long-term and short-term issues that we all need to work on together,” Williams said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure entries.title: SBA offering low-interest loans to residents, businesses impacted by last year's severe storms
entries.summary: <p>Residents and businesses in six counties, including Cook, that were affected by severe storms and flooding last year can apply for low-interest federal loans for expenses not covered by insurance, the U.S. Small Business Administration announced earlier this month.</p><p>The loans are available following the SBA’s disaster declaration, which came after a request from Gov. JB Pritzker on Mar. 12, an SBA news release said.</p><p>The loans are for damages related to the storms that took place Aug. 16-19, 2025, a month when some Chicago residents saw their <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/weather/2025/08/19/widespread-torrential-rainfall-leaves-several-inches-after-thunderstorms-sweep-through-chicago" >homes flooded twice</a>. Residents of Cook, DuPage, Kane, Lake, McHenry and Will counties are eligible to apply.</p><p>The two types of SBA loans available are the Physical Damage Loans for homeowners, renters, businesses and nonprofits and Economic Injury Disaster Loans for businesses and nonprofits. Loan terms are a maximum of 30 years. Businesses with credit available elsewhere are able to receive a max seven-year term.</p><p>Homeowners can apply for up to $500,000 to repair or replace their homes. For both homeowners and renters, they can apply for up to $100,000 for damages to personal property such as furniture, appliances and cars.</p><p>Interest rates are as low as 3% for homeowners and renters, about 3.6% for nonprofits and 4% for businesses. Payments are not due and interest does not accrue until 12 months from the date of the first loan disbursement, according to the SBA. The loan amounts and terms are set based on each applicant’s financial situation.</p><p>Businesses and some nonprofits are eligible to borrow up to $2 million for physical damages to assets such as machinery, equipment and inventory. Even if a business or nonprofit didn’t suffer physical damages, it can still apply for loans to cover fixed debts, payroll costs and other bills not paid due to the disaster.</p><p>The SBA has opened Disaster Loan Outreach Centers in Chicago, suburban Cook County and online to help residents receive assistance when applying for the loans.</p><p>Borrowers have until May 15 to file applications for physical property damage and until Dec. 16 to file applications for economic injury. Applicants must have social security numbers to be eligible.</p><p>To apply for the loans, applicants can visit the SBA’s website at <a class="Link" href="https://www.sba.gov/funding-programs/disaster-assistance" target="_blank" >sba.gov/disaster</a>.</p>
entries.author: Lizzie Kane

entries.title: Bears GM Ryan Poles eyes draft picks to remake defense in coordinator's image
entries.summary: <p>PHOENIX — <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> coach Ben Johnson tried to argue <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/halas-intrigue/2026/03/30/bears-nfl-annual-meetings-podcast-halas-intrigue" target="_blank" >Monday morning</a> his roster was deep enough that the team could afford to draft the best available player next month.</p><p>“I think we can go any direction we need to, and stay true to that,” he said, “and feel pretty good about it.”</p><p>Deep down, he must know better. So must general manager Ryan Poles. Because this year’s draft cries out for the Bears to draft a defensive player in Round 1. And maybe two more in Round 2.</p><p>It’s essential to the team’s future on and off the field. They haven’t picked a first-round defender since Roquan Smith in 2018 and are set to pay $166 million, the fourth-most in the NFL, to their 2026 defense alone. That’s unsustainable. The Bears need an influx in young defensive talent on the field who collect a reasonable paycheck off it.</p><p>“We talk about that a lot,” Poles said Monday at the NFL’s annual meeting.</p><p>Poles just told the world what he, Johnson and defensive coordinator Dennis Allen thought of the team’s defense. He traded linebacker Tremaine Edmunds and let starting safeties Kevin Byard and Jaquan Brisker and cornerback Nahshon Wright leave via free agency. No NFL team gave any of their defensive backs more than a one-year deal.</p><p>The Bears swapped them out for players who they think better fit Allen’s defensive mindset — but need to add more in the draft.</p><p>“If you go back and see the teams that D.A. has had, specifically the defenses in New Orleans, there’s an aggressiveness, there’s a violence and there’s a speed that they play with,” Poles said. “So we’re trying to match that …</p><p></p><p>“That’s part of our evaluation process with the rookie class — prioritizing the guys that fit perfectly or that kind of borderline fit and need some development. And then we really try to push aside the guys that don’t fit.”</p><p>The hard part about drafting someone who fits Allen’s prototype is that the Bears are picking so late in Round 1. Poles looks to two late-round stalwarts for inspiration — the Steelers and Ravens. Since 2000, there have been 109 seasons in which a defensive player drafted between 20<sup>th</sup> and 32<sup>nd</sup> overall made the Pro Bowl. The Ravens are responsible for 11 — nine for safety Ed Reed and two for linebacker Patrick Queen. The Steelers claim 14 — eight for edge rusher TJ Watt and six for defensive tackle Cam Heyward.</p><p>Bears defensive end Montez Sweat is another. So is Cam Jordan, Allen’s former standout edge rusher in New Orleans. But those defensive linemen were the exception, not the rule.</p><p>“For the D-line it gets more difficult as you get further along in the draft,” Poles said. “You’re definitely going to have to develop (them).”</p><p>That's been a problem for the Bears for a generation. Acquiring them has gone better — Khalil Mack and Julius Peppers were stars — but proved only to be a patch. Maxx Crosby seems to be staying with the Raiders after the asking price of two first-round picks was too rich for Poles’ liking.</p><p>There’s nothing wrong with taking a high-floor player at No. 25 rather than someone with “this mythical high ceiling that maybe you never reach,” Poles said. Miami’s Rueben Bain Jr. and Texas A&M’s Cashius Howell, two of the most productive edge rushers in college football, have short arms that could hurt their draft status.</p><p>“Just a good football player that plays with the right mentality — a high motor, can overcome their short-comings, whether they have short arms or it’s a height thing or size thing,” Poles said. “That’s what we’re going to be taking into consideration.”</p><p></p><p>So long as it’s a defensive player.</p><p></p><p>“If all things are equal, that usually clinches it in a way, in terms of our needs,” Poles said. “But we need to be true to the board and true to the talent to make sure they truly are equal.”</p><p></p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" entries.title: Bears coach Ben Johnson embraces celebrity stature after spectacular turnaround season
entries.summary: <p>PHOENIX — Few Chicago sports figures have been as popular as <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> coach <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2025/09/01/the-bears-and-their-fans-needed-a-truth-teller-they-got-one-in-new-coach-ben-johnson" >Ben Johnson</a>, perhaps their most celebrated leader since Mike Ditka, and their most successful season in over a decade vaulted his fame to a new height.</p><p>Johnson was well known in NFL circles and among Lions fans during his run as their offensive coordinator before taking this job, but not like this. Nothing beats the energy of a thrilling Bears season, and Johnson fueled it with fiery post-game speeches that peaked with him ripping his shirt off after beating the Eagles to trigger a hotdog giveaway at Wiener’s Circle.</p><p>As high as his status has soared over the past year, though, he’s still living a fairly normal life. Crowds went wild when he was shown recently at Blackhawks, Bulls and Cubs game, but he often goes unnoticed, including Sunday morning at O’Hare Airport while waiting for his flight to Phoenix for the NFL Annual Meeting.</p><p>“Someone else from the team was flying out, too, and I’m standing at the gate, and he texted me, ‘I can’t believe nobody is bothering you right now,’” Johnson said Monday in an interview with the Sun-Times at the Arizona Biltmore. “I fly right under the radar, so it’s all good.”</p><p>That’ll happen less and less the longer he coaches the Bears, whether it goes well or not. So far, coming off an 11-6 season, a playoff win over the Packers and free hotdogs, the attention has been positive.</p><p>Johnson was suited to be the front man for that, and the Bears badly needed it after predecessor Matt Eberflus’ dull demeanor and perpetual missteps. Johnson was regarded as a brilliant offensive mind, but brought an unexpected, genuine intensity, too.</p><p>He wasn’t playing a character, for example, in those raucous locker room scenes when players erupted as he pulled off his shirt or yelled, “[Expletive] the Packers… I [expletive] hate those guys.” He fed off the vibe of the moment, and it resonated.</p><p>“Nothing that happens in that locker room is done for show,” Johnson said. “It’s entirely for our players. That’s how I’ve always seen it, and that won’t change.”</p><p>He smirked.</p><p>“Maybe I should be a little bit more aware that there are cameras in there,” he said.</p><p>Hopefully not.</p><p>The spotlight is relatively new to Johnson, 39, who was a walk-on quarterback at North Carolina and relatively unknown assistant coach who rose to the coordinator level only four years ago. He married his high school sweetheart. They have a young family, and he’s been doing his best to “sync” with them now that he’s in the lighter part of his work calendar.</p><p>Other than being an NFL head coach, he’s a regular guy who became a somewhat of an overnight celebrity in the country’s third-largest market.</p><p>“The best thing about this city is there’s so much that it offers: restaurants, museums, sporting events,” Johnson said. “But as a result, there is a little more recognition, and that comes with the territory.</p><p>“People have been incredibly respectful of my family time. But I appreciate that we live in a city that loves their NFL team. That’s a pretty cool thing. I also know that when things don’t go well, they’re going to let us know about it. It just so happened that they felt like the year went pretty well last year, so there’s a lot of positive feedback coming right now.”</p><p>That’s modest. No Chicago coach or manager could touch Johnson’s approval rating.</p><p>While he didn’t seek stardom, he seems comfortable with it. He also has a healthy grasp of how fast it can flip on him if the team starts sliding. Matt Nagy won Coach of the Year in 2018, then fans chanted “Fire Nagy” at his son’s high school football game as the Bears spiraled in 2021.</p><p>It’s always easier at the beginning, when expectations are at their lowest, and they were rarely as low as they were coming out of the Eberflus debacle. Johnson won’t be playing with house money anymore. The Bears were good last season, and now, rightfully, the demand is for them to become great.</p><p>“People outside the building are going to have a win total projection and be talking Super Bowls and all that, and that’s their business,” Johnson said. “We go into our team meeting and set our goals and we’re going to stick to them. It’s really consistent with how we’ve operated.”</p><p>A little over a year into the job, Johnson has been fantastic with the dual roles of running the team and being the face of it. The Bears have occasionally found someone good at one or the other, but rarely both. He has been exactly what they needed, and he’s only getting started.</p>
entries.author: Jason Lieser

entries.title: Cubs right fielder Seiya Suzuki 'at the last steps' of recovery from PCL sprain
entries.summary: <p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" >Cubs</a> right fielder <b>Seiya Suzuki</b> is inching closer to a return from his PCL sprain in the World Baseball Classic.</p><p>Manager <b>Craig Counsell</b> said that Suzuki might return on the Cubs’ six-game road trip that begins Friday in Cleveland. Counsell said the next two days are critical for determining if Suzuki is ready for a rehab assignment.</p><p>“He's got to pass two more tests, and [Tuesday] will probably be the bigger day in terms of workload,” Counsell said.</p><p>Suzuki has made progress each day in his recovery. The Cubs could use Suzuki, who hit 32 homers and 103 RBIs with a .804 OPS last season, back in the lineup and in right field.</p><p>“We're at the last steps,” Counsell said. “We're at basically full speed, like quick change of directions, quick stops, things like that.”</p><p>The Cubs have been adamant about ensuring Suzuki was healthy enough to return to the field. They didn’t want him to return earlier than necessary and risk compromising himself for the rest of the season. Counsell said the club “very much wants him to be capable of playing right field when he comes back.”</p><h3><b>Cabrera’s debut</b></h3><h3></h3><p>Right-hander <b>Edward Cabrera </b>made his Cubs debut Monday night against the Angels. All-Star <b>Alex Bregman</b> garnered the most headlines after he signed his five-year, $175 million contract, but Cabrera will be just as important to a roster that lacked enough starting pitching in the National League Division Series against the Brewers.</p><p>Cabrera’s average fastball velocity (96.9 mph) was in the 87th percentile. His whiff rate of 29.4% was in the 78th percentile. He brings velocity with swing-and-miss in his arsenal that differs from the other Cubs starters who rely on ground balls and soft contact.</p><p>To compete and be a contender in the majors, you have to have a starting staff that can strike out opposing hitters. But acquiring those pitchers is either expensive or requires significant prospect capital. That the Cubs traded former top outfield prospect <b>Owen Caissie</b> indicates the expectations they have that Cabrera can help them get over the hump.</p><p>The Cubs have grown adept at maximizing a pitcher’s strengths by tinkering with their arsenals or delivery, but Counsell quelled any notion that they have to fix anything with Cabrera, who allowed a 3.53 ERA last season.</p><p>“We acquired a good pitcher,” Counsell said. “We're not trying to do much here. We're just trying to get a good pitcher healthy and make as many starts as he can make. There's not a lot of changes here.”</p><p>But Cabrera has embraced this next chapter of his career with the Cubs after spending five years with the Marlins.</p><p>“He has treated being a Cub as a real new start to his career,” Counsell said.</p><p></p><h3><b>Catcher rotation</b></h3><h3></h3><p></p><p><b>Carson Kelly</b> got the nod at catcher for the Cubs on Monday. With <b>Miguel Amaya</b> healthy, the Cubs have a formidable tandem that is both adept defensively and at the plate.</p><p></p><p>How Counsell balances playing time will be watched throughout the season. Last season was one of Kelly’s most productive. He batted .249 with 17 homers and 50 RBIs. Amaya appeared in just 28 games last season because of an oblique injury and an ankle sprain.</p><p></p><bsp-listcarousel class="PageListE" data-module data-column-count=3 data-hide-authors="true" entries.author: Kyle Williams

entries.title: Kindergartners learn to bike on new wheels at Chicago elementary school
entries.summary: <p>Students at Sidney Sawyer Elementary School in Chicago’s Gage Park neighborhood received a fleet of 24 bikes on Monday.</p><p>For some of the students, it was their first time on a bike at all.</p><p>“I was a little nervous because it was my first time,” said kindergartener Monsé Maldonado. “I thought I was gonna drop myself, but I didn't. It turned out good.”</p><p>Attorneys from the law firm Motorcycle Safety Lawyers helped raise the money for the nonprofit All Kids Bike, which donated the equipment. The law firm says they work with underprivileged schools who have the space to teach a bike education program. The school will also receive a curriculum to teach kids how to ride.</p><p>The kids will first learn using the balance bike method. They’ll focus on balance and steering first without using the pedals.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a entries.author: Candace Dane Chambers

entries.title: Onward Neighborhood House breaks ground on Belmont Cragin community center
entries.summary: <p>Developers broke ground Monday on a Belmont Cragin community center that will help close the neighborhood’s gap in offering residents access to health and human services.</p><p>Onward Neighborhood House was joined by project and community partners for the groundbreaking ceremony to celebrate what will become its premier community services center. </p><p>When CARES Community Center opens at 2644 N. Central Ave. before the end of the year, it will expand access to health care, improve food security and offer other wraparound services for immigrant and low-income families in Belmont Cragin.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-500000" name="image-500000"></a> <picture 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f44de3d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+44/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb9%2Fe2%2F59a1a6fd40a3955f0cbf6184f38f%2Fonward-260331-23.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ee82a93/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+44/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb9%2Fe2%2F59a1a6fd40a3955f0cbf6184f38f%2Fonward-260331-23.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Onward Neighborhood House Executive Director Mario Garcia speaks about the nonprofit’s future CARES Community Center at 2644 N. Central Ave. in Belmont Cragin.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“This is a project that … will bring access to health, health care, food, education and also, more importantly, especially these times of polarization, is going to have space for communities to come together,” Mario Garcia, executive director of Onward Neighborhood House, said.</p><p>Founded in West Town in 1893, Onward Neighborhood House is a nonprofit that aids immigrants and underserved families and provides free access to adult education, food and child care. </p><p>The volunteer-based health clinic CommunityHealth will open a co-location clinic at the center with IWS Family Health, which is a federally-qualified health center look-alike. </p><p>The CARES Community Center will also include bilingual mental health services through a partnership with Cicero Family Service.</p><p>There will be an expanded food pantry, adult education classrooms, an Illinois Welcoming Center for immigrants and refugees and open community space.</p><p>The center has been years in the making, though the vision has evolved over time.</p><p>The building was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2022/10/14/23404739/immigrant-organization-northwest-new-space-open-house-belmont-cragin-onwards-logan-foundation" >donated by the Reva and David Logan Foundation</a> in 2022. The foundation, who started working with Onward in 2021, found the site and approached Onward about using it as a food pantry — one of the nonprofit’s largest programs.</p><p>But the partners saw the need for additional programs and services in Belmont Cragin, Emilio Araujo, director of development and communications for Onward, said. The vision then grew to include the welcoming center, medical clinic and other services.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-450000" name="image-450000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/78d759b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+253/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3f%2Fec%2F687dd75943c8905b3f4008cecdbd%2Fonward-260331-08.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/985aa88/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+253/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3f%2Fec%2F687dd75943c8905b3f4008cecdbd%2Fonward-260331-08.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e702ffe/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+253/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3f%2Fec%2F687dd75943c8905b3f4008cecdbd%2Fonward-260331-08.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="An open space under construction with exposed ceilings, ladders against the brick walls and other construction equipment." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e702ffe/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+253/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3f%2Fec%2F687dd75943c8905b3f4008cecdbd%2Fonward-260331-08.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3436f52/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+253/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3f%2Fec%2F687dd75943c8905b3f4008cecdbd%2Fonward-260331-08.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e702ffe/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+253/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3f%2Fec%2F687dd75943c8905b3f4008cecdbd%2Fonward-260331-08.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Inside the future CARES Community Center in Belmont Cragin.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Belmont Cragin is one of the largest immigrant communities in Chicago, and 79.1% of the population is Hispanic, according to 2025 data from the Chicago Metropolitan Agency for Planning. It also faces some of the highest barriers to accessing health care.</p><p>Seventeen percent of Belmont Cragin residents are uninsured — nearly double the rate for Chicago, according to Onward. The neighborhood has 30.4 primary care providers for every 100,000 residents, compared to 107.7 citywide.</p><p>“For years, we've been witnessing the destruction of brick-and-mortar infrastructure that once offered Chicago citizens places of safety, times of need, places where people can be, places where they can access the services they need,” Richard Logan, president of the Reva and David Logan Foundation, said. </p><p>“With the disappearance of many of these venues in neighborhoods across the city, our communities have become less equitable, more isolated and much less able to provide hope and take care of the most vulnerable around us. … When this building came on the market, we came [to] Onward Neighborhood House, guided by our internal mantra: invest in the jockey and not the horse.”</p><p>Onward has longstanding partnerships that have helped create a web of services, Araujo said. CARES Community Center is the manifestation of that and will span 31,000 square feet. It's also blocks away from the nonprofit’s space on West Diversey Avenue.</p><p>The site used to be a funeral home, according to Araujo. Onward has a long-term lease for the space, which the foundation purchased for $1.3 million in 2022, according to property records.</p><p>Monday marked the start of construction after interior demolition at the former funeral home was completed. Araujo said the nonprofit hopes to hold a grand opening in September, once construction is done.</p><p>The community center is a $7.5 million project. In 2024, Onward received a $3 million federal grant to support the center’s development. The project also received nearly $1.5 million in state funding and a $500,000 grant from the city of Chicago, according to Araujo.</p>
entries.author: Abby Miller

entries.title: Selective prosecution claims in 'Broadview Six' case the result of 'fevered paranoia,' feds say
entries.summary: <p>Federal prosecutors in Chicago delivered a full-throated defense Monday<b> </b>of the conspiracy case leveled against the so-called “Broadview Six,”<b> </b>insisting that any claim of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/13/chicago-trump-abughazaleh-justice-department-broadview-midway-blitz-ice" target="_blank" >selective or vindictive prosecution</a> is “the product of fevered paranoia and delusional speculation.”</p><p>They mocked the notion that White House officials “had such an interest in local Illinois government”<b> </b>to pick “politically obscure figures”<b> </b>like Kat<b> </b>Abughazaleh<b> </b>out of a crowd, “order their selective and vindictive prosecution, and blithely expect career prosecutors in Chicago to violate multiple ethical and legal standards.”</p><p>They also seemed to take such accusations personally, complaining that “the unavoidable conclusions to be drawn” are that officials in Chicago’s U.S. attorney’s office “have not only acted in bad faith but have committed prosecutorial misconduct.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/19/broadview-six-ice-trump-doj-justice-chicago-midway-blitz" target="_blank" >‘Broadview Six’ ICE protesters can see White House communications about their case — if they exist</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/16/chicago-broadview-six-kat-abughazaleh-midway-blitz-trump-ice" target="_blank" >Remaining ‘Broadview Six’ defendants want conspiracy charge tossed, argue protesting isn’t a crime</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Ultimately, prosecutors insisted that “there are no communications of any nature from, to, or with anyone” outside the U.S. attorney’s office regarding investigative or charging decisions in the case, aside from local FBI investigators.</p><p>“To be crystal clear, this specifically includes anyone in the White House and all components and offices of Main Justice,” Assistant U.S. Attorney William<b> </b>Hogan<b> </b>wrote in Monday’s 21-page court filing.</p><p>Hogan’s commentary came in the conspiracy case against a group of activists who protested outside the U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement facility<b> </b>in Broadview last fall, during the Trump administration’s Operation Midway Blitz deportation campaign.</p><p>The feds originally charged six of the activists but <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/12/chicago-midway-blitz-immigration-trump-kat-abughazaleh-sharp-joselyn-walsh-broadview-six" target="_blank" >dropped charges</a> against two. Still charged are former congressional candidate Abughazaleh, Oak Park village trustee Brian Straw, 45th Ward Democratic committeeperson Michael Rabbitt and Andre Martin, a member of Abughazaleh’s campaign staff.</p><p>All four are involved in local Democratic politics.</p><p>The four are accused of a conspiracy to impede a federal agent. But defense attorneys have argued the indictment is another example of President Donald Trump’s administration using the Justice Department to target his “perceived political enemies.”</p><p>Defense attorneys have until Thursday to respond to Hogan’s court filing. Both sides are due back in U.S. District Judge April Perry’s courtroom<b> </b>April 7. The case is set for trial May 26.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-4c0000" name="image-4c0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/844c743/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F35%2Ff5%2Fabe4ba2b44f1b2989a0f9c14d81f%2Fbviewsix-260320-05.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7ac024c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F35%2Ff5%2Fabe4ba2b44f1b2989a0f9c14d81f%2Fbviewsix-260320-05.JPG 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5d36bfd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F35%2Ff5%2Fabe4ba2b44f1b2989a0f9c14d81f%2Fbviewsix-260320-05.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Oak Park Village Trustee Brian Straw leaves the Dirksen Federal Courthouse after a hearing in the so-called "Broadview Six" case on Thursday, March 19, 2026." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5d36bfd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F35%2Ff5%2Fabe4ba2b44f1b2989a0f9c14d81f%2Fbviewsix-260320-05.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/50f25ad/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F35%2Ff5%2Fabe4ba2b44f1b2989a0f9c14d81f%2Fbviewsix-260320-05.JPG 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5d36bfd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F35%2Ff5%2Fabe4ba2b44f1b2989a0f9c14d81f%2Fbviewsix-260320-05.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Oak Park Village Trustee Brian Straw leaves the Dirksen Federal Courthouse after a hearing in the so-called “Broadview Six” case on Thursday, March 19, 2026.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The charges revolve around events on the morning of Sept. 26 outside the ICE facility. The indictment alleges that, while a federal agent drove a vehicle toward the facility, the defendants and others surrounded it. Members of the larger crowd allegedly banged on the vehicle, pushed against it, scratched it and even etched the word “PIG” into it.</p><p>The crowd allegedly broke a side mirror and a rear windshield wiper and forced the agent “to drive at an extremely slow rate of speed.”</p><p>Defense attorneys say the agent did not wait for police to clear a path, did not identify himself and did not order the crowd to move aside.</p><p>Pushing back on the claims of selective prosecution, Hogan wrote Monday<b> </b>that videos "clearly show that most individuals who swarmed and impeded" the vehicle “could not be identified because they were wearing masks or other coverings that obscured their faces.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/16/chicago-broadview-six-kat-abughazaleh-midway-blitz-trump-ice" >Remaining ‘Broadview Six’ defendants want conspiracy charge tossed, argue protesting isn’t a crime</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>But the feds “faced no comparable difficulty in identifying the original six defendants, each of whom was unmasked,” Hogan wrote. Some of them also made public statements confirming their presence at the facility during the incident, he added.</p><p>Even when the FBI positively identified others, prosecutors made the final call on who to charge “based on the strength of the evidence,”<b> </b>Hogan explained. They chose not to bring charges against a Chicago alderman, a “quasi-public figure who is well known”<b> </b>for protesting in Broadview, a prominent attorney and several others,<b> </b>the prosecutor added.</p><p>Hogan did not identify those individuals by name.</p><p>“Defendants have presented no evidence that individuals similarly situated to them were treated differently or that there was any improper purpose in the decision to charge them for the simple reason that there is no such evidence,” Hogan wrote.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe src="https://docs.google.com/viewer?embedded=true&url=https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/7b/73/8d13d19c425e81198b6bae06e586/usavrabbittcompelresponse.pdf"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p></p>
entries.author: Jon Seidel

entries.title: Jeff Blashill is right to use Blackhawks' final games for experimentation
entries.summary: <p>It was strange to see some <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a> fans criticizing coach Jeff Blashill on Sunday for experimenting with the team's lineup.</p><p>In fact, that's exactly what Blashill should be doing right now.</p><p>The final weeks of this season are essentially a trial run for the Hawks' young core, most of which has — at long last — reached the NHL and taken over the roster.</p><p>Come next season, especially once one last crucial piece in Roman Kantserov arrives, expectations will begin to rise. It will be time for the Hawks' ascension to begin in earnest.</p><p>The final weeks of this season, however, are the perfect time for experimentation. The kids are all present, but the Hawks are miles out of the playoff race. Losing isn't worth much heartache, considering it will help the organization harvest one more top-five draft pick from this rebuild.</p><p>To make a pottery analogy, the clay is already on the wheel but the kiln hasn't begun to heat yet. Blashill is free to attempt to mold any type of bowl he likes; there will be no consequences if it falls apart in his hands.</p><p>Will Anton Frondell succeed as a center, rather than a winger, in the NHL? Nobody knows, including Blashill. The odds are perhaps against it. But why not find out?</p><p>"He hasn’t played center all year, so it’s probably not the easiest position to put him in — I recognize that," Blashill said Sunday. "But he’s played center a lot in his life, and I think he’s got traits to be a really good 200-foot winning center. And I would like to get a look at it this year rather than at the beginning of next year, so that if that’s the way we go, we can hit the ground running.</p><p>"It's going to put a lot [of responsibilities] on his shoulders, and it might affect his game a little bit until he gets really comfortable. But I want to look at it anyways."</p><p>Will a five-forward power play quarterbacked by <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/03/30/blackhawks-connor-bedard-nhl-creating-space-nikita-kucherov-jeff-blashill" target="_blank" >Connor Bedard</a> work? Again, nobody knows, including Blashill — but that's fine.</p><p>"It’s easier to get the puck in [Bedard's] hands up top than it is on the flank," Blashill explained. "He’s talked about wanting that opportunity to do it. He’s obviously a really smart player. He’s got good deception. We thought, 'Let’s give it a try and see it.' It’s something we've talked about through the year."</p><p>Frondell and the power play turned out to be two of the Hawks' brighter spots in their <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/03/29/blackhawks-devils-spencer-knight-frank-nazar-defensive-issues" target="_blank" >dramatic loss Sunday</a> against the Devils.</p><p>The Swedish rookie earned two assists and now touts four points in his first four NHL games. His ability to force turnovers and create havoc around the opponents' nets has stood out in particular.</p><p>He did get caught in no man's land as the "F3" (third forward) on the failed Hawks forecheck that directly preceded Jack Hughes' game-winning goal. But those kinds of lessons are better learned now than in October, at least.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Frondell forces a turnover setting Bedard up with a golden chance: <a href="https://t.co/UYjjASMAnN">pic.twitter.com/UYjjASMAnN</a></p>— BHF (@BlackhawksFocus) <a href="https://twitter.com/BlackhawksFocus/status/2037673276105724075?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 27, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>The power play, meanwhile, generated one goal — its first in a nine-game span — and a number of dangerous looks on three opportunities. </p><p>Bedard looked comfortable up top, and his accurate passing was effective from that position. Any improvement is welcome; the power play ranks last in the NHL since Jan. 11 with a 9.5% conversion rate.</p><p>Safe to say, those two ideas deserve runways to accumulate larger samples of data, as have any other half-reasonable ideas the coaching staff can brainstorm.</p><p>On a related note, it would be swell to see prospect goalie Drew Commesso make another NHL start or two down the stretch, if he is to compete with Arvid Soderblom for the full-time backup role in camp next fall. </p><p>April's purpose is getting a sense of what the Hawks might be able to build with these puzzle pieces.</p><p></p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" id="list-890005" name="list-890005"></a><div class="PageList-header" > <div class="PageList-header-title-wrap" > <div class="PageList-header-title"> <a class="" entries.author: Ben Pope

entries.title: White Sox players calling ‘em like they feel ‘em under MLB’s new ABS challenge system
entries.summary: <p>MIAMI — The first few games of the automated ball/strike era have been a mixed bag for the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" ><u>White Sox</u></a>.</p><p>Catcher <b>Edgar Quero</b> hasn’t been shy about questioning umpires under the new ABS challenge system implemented <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/sports-media/2026/02/12/how-abs-challenge-system-will-affect-mlb-tv-broadcasts-chicago-cubs-white-sox-spring-training-schedules" target="_blank" ><u>this season</u></a>, but Sox hitters still are feeling it out.</p><p>‘‘We’ve seen them less willing to pull the trigger on some challenges,’’ manager <b>Will Venable</b> said. ‘‘And I don’t get instant feedback on all these pitches on where they’re at, but it seems like we’re seeing less challenges from hitters.’’</p><p>Among the Sox’ hitters, only <b>Lenyn Sosa</b> and <b>Tristan Peters</b> called for reviews in Milwaukee. Both lost, and Peters’ challenge wasn’t particularly close.</p><p>Quero challenged seven calls from behind the plate in the first three games, getting four bad ones overturned. No Sox pitcher initiated a review during the first series.</p><p>So should the Sox be quicker to tap their hat or helmet?</p><p>‘‘If they’re right, yeah,’’ Venable said with a laugh. ‘‘We want to make sure we’re challenging the right pitches. . . . It’s still new for a lot of these guys.’’</p><p>Venable didn’t tip the Sox’ ABS playbook, but he said ‘‘a mid-at-bat challenge is different than a potential strikeout or walk at-bat-ending challenge. There’s different things, different parts of the zone that we’ve talked about, different guidelines.’’</p><p>Quero said he’ll keep going with his gut.</p><p>‘‘Yeah, [based on] good feelings and . . . seeing the ball, where it’s coming from, and knowing the strike zone a little bit better,’’ he said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>John Fisher/Getty Images</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>Long road back</b></h3><p>Left-hander <b>Anthony Kay’s</b> start Sunday in Milwaukee was his first big-league action in 2½ years. He joined the Sox on <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/02/19/white-sox-anthony-kay-looks-to-carry-over-success-from-japan" target="_blank" ><u>a two-year, $12 million contract</u></a> during the offseason after rejuvenating his career with a stint in Japan.</p><p>‘‘I’ve just grown so much mentally those couple of years in Japan,’’ Kay, 31, said. ‘‘That’s been a huge difference for me in my career.’’</p><p></p><p>It wasn’t pretty, but Kay turned in the best start of the Sox’ abysmal opening series, giving up two runs and three hits, striking out five and walking four in 4⅔ innings.</p><p></p><p>‘‘Definitely got a little emotional walking out to the bullpen before the game,’’ Kay said. ‘‘Definitely was a little amped up and had to hone that in a little bit. I think I did a good job of settling in after that.’’</p><p></p><p>It wasn’t exactly the season-opening sensation the Sox have gotten from a certain other Japanese veteran on their roster, but they are relying on Kay to eat up some serious innings this season while waiting for a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/02/12/noah-schultz-hagen-smith-white-sox-spring-training-triple-a" target="_blank" ><u>a wave of talented arms</u></a> to emerge from Triple-A Charlotte.</p><p></p><p>Kay got a preview of new teammate <b>Munetaka Murakami’s</b> prodigious power while facing him overseas.</p><p></p><p>‘‘I’m not surprised by it,’’ Kay said of Murakami’s <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/29/white-sox-colson-montgomery-grand-slam-murakami-homer" target="_blank" ><u>three home runs</u></a> in his first three MLB games. ‘‘I know some people are, but I’ve seen it.’’</p><p></p><h3>Numbers game</h3><p></p><p>Before Opening Day, Quero switched his jersey number from the 7 he debuted with last season to 26, which he said holds personal significance as a combination of the birthdates of his mother, his sister and his own.</p><p></p><p>It’s also the number catcher <b>Korey Lee</b> wore for the Sox until being designated for assignment at the end of spring training, ending up as <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/22/white-sox-sign-catcher-reese-mcguire-leaving-korey-lee-as-odd-man-out" target="_blank" ><u>the odd man out</u></a> when general manager <b>Chris Getz</b> signed superior pitch-framer <b>Reese McGuire</b>.</p><p></p><p>Lee cleared waivers and now is wearing No. 26 at Charlotte.</p><div class="PageListL" data-module data-hide-authors="true" entries.title: First blood drive at Bronzeville clinic highlights need for diverse donor pool
entries.summary: <p>Sharon Brooks spent part of Monday afternoon donating blood. The 65-year-old said she used to be anemic.</p><p>Now better, she's returning the favor to those who donated blood to her.</p><p>“I was able to get blood to build my blood up,” said Brooks, of Chicago. “That’s why I want to give, because I want to give back to those that are in need of the blood type that I have."</p><p>The American Red Cross partnered with Northwestern Medicine to host the first community blood drive at the recently opened Northwestern Medicine Bronzeville Outpatient Center, 4822 S. Cottage Grove Ave.</p><p>The event in Bronzeville, a predominantly African American neighborhood, highlights a need for a more diverse blood donor base, said Tammy Winchester, emerging markets manager for the American Red Cross of Illinois.</p><p>“Right now, we don't have enough diverse donors — diverse meaning African Americans, Latinos, Asian Americans," Winchester said. "This is our effort to march forward and educate the community to start participating in this ... call of action.”</p><p>Donated blood is essential for patients undergoing surgery, cancer treatment, organ transplants and trauma care. Blood is also needed for people living with chronic illnesses such as sickle cell disease. Those conditions often require closely matched blood types.</p><p>Sickle cell disease affects about 100,000 people in the United States, according to the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. More than 90% of people with sickle cell disease are Black.</p><p>Some people choose not to donate blood due to lack of education, lack of access or fear of needles, Winchester said. But she says the need for blood donors should overcome those fears.</p><p>“I always tell people I don't like needles, but I try to donate," she said. "We can't say it enough about the need for diverse donors and to build our donor pool."</p><p>Kimbra Bell, director of the Northwestern Medicine Bronzeville Outpatient Center, said people are saving lives when they donate blood.</p><p>“When community members come together to donate blood, they are quite literally helping save lives — whether it’s a neighbor undergoing surgery, a child with sickle cell disease or a trauma patient in urgent need," Bell said.</p>
entries.author: Michael Puente
